The experimental findings indicate that multimodal approach is much better than the unimodal systems, and early fusion is the most accurate. The predominant role of the GSR amplitude and eye fixation measures is pointed out by explainability analysis and is expected according to the existing psychophysiological theories. The suggested architecture is strong, transparent, and has prospective applications in the real world like affect-aware systems, mental health monitoring, and adaptive human-computer interaction.

title: Framework for Explainable Learning in Federated 3D Multimodal Cancer Detection
authors: D.Nagadurga Naveen, N.Ankith Yadav, P. Rajalakshmi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: The uneven nature of the multimodal data, absence of sizable, centralized datasets brought on by privacy and data-sharing regulations across healthcare organizations make it difficult to diagnose cancer early using medical imaging. Traditional deep learning models frequently use centralized training and single-modality data, which restricts generalization and puts private patient data at risk. In order to get over these restrictions, this research suggests a Federated 3D Multimodal Explainable Learning Framework that uses distributed 3D medical imaging and clinical data to enable collaborative and privacy-preserving cancer diagnosis. The framework enables efficient learning from multimodal inputs like CT, MRI, and patient metadata by integrating Transformer-based encoders for cross-modal feature fusion and Convolutional neural networks(CNNs) in three dimensions for the extraction of spatial features. A federated aggregation strategy (FedAvg) synchronizes local models from many medical institutions without sharing raw data, protecting patient privacy.Additionally, by emphasizing the important areas and characteristics affecting the diagnosis, explainable AI modules like Grad-CAM and attention visualization offer interpretability. According to experimental assessment, the suggested strategy offers transparency and complies with healthcare privacy regulations while outperforming conventional centralized and unimodal methods for accuracy, F1-score, and resilience. This technique creates a new path for safe and explicable AI-based cancer detection by bridging the gaps between data privacy, multimodal integration, and model interpretability.

title: Personalized Healthcare Assistant for Lung Cancer Detection Using Hybrid Machine Learning Approach
authors: M Sitha Ram, Bhanu Chander Balusa, Phaneendra Kanakamedala, Vejendla Meenakshi, Chikoti Trisha, Allada Shankar, Vemuri Praveena
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Lung cancer is one of the main cause of cancer related death globally so the early detection techniques are really essential for reducing the deaths globally every year. In order to diagnose lung cancer, our research proposes a hybrid machine learning technique which uses XGBoost for tabular data analysis and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for histopathological image classification. The suggested approach makes use of two datasets: a histopathological image dataset with 25,000 images of lung cancer, and a lung cancer dataset with 15 clinical and demographic variables. While the CNN model showed 91.06% training and validation accuracy for image classification whereas the XGBoost model achieved 98.39% accuracy for tabular data set. we used Streamlit to create a web based tool for this research project for real time prediction. The hybrid approach is great for early lung cancer screening programs and early lung cancer detection and support systems.

title: MooTrack360: A Novel Fisheye Camera Dataset for Robust Multi Dairy Cow Detection and Tracking
authors: Rasmus Gjerlund K. Christiansen, Toan Van Nguyen, Lasse Rose Malskær, Leon Bodenhagen, Dirk Kraft
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: MooTrack360 is a novel top-down fisheye dataset designed to support the development of robust camera surveillance and monitoring systems in large-scale, real-world environments. While centered around continuous livestock monitoring of Holstein dairy cows, the dataset addresses general challenges in computer vision such as fisheye distortion, overlapping fields of view, variable lighting, and occlusions. It includes 102,747 annotated cow instances across 1,500 images, each labelled as "standing" or "lying", along with a 1-hour annotated video sequence for tracking evaluation. Several unannotated sequences are included for development and analysis. The dataset enables detection and tracking under conditions ranging from daylight to infrared-assisted nighttime imaging, and facilitates both application-specific and generalized model evaluation. A calibration pipeline based on the Double Sphere Camera model is provided to support distortion correction and precise spatial localization. An ac companying end-to-end training framework further addresses challenges such as illumination changes and occlusions. Benchmarks using state-of-the-art detection and tracking methods demonstrate the dataset’s potential to advance research in non-invasive, camera-based monitoring across domains. The complete dataset—including annotated images, tracking sequences, sample videos, calibration footage, and the full codebase, is available at: https://zenodo.org/records/15470268.

title: Enhanced Liver Tumor Segmentation and Feature Extraction Using Gaussian Filtering and LICA on CT Images
authors: D. Anandkumar, S. John Justin Thangaraj, C. Alamelu, M. Vallikkannu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: Liver cancer is among the major causes of cancer related deaths in the world and this is partly caused by the late stages of diagnosis and inability to detect it early through effective means. Effective segmentation and sound feature detection of liver CT images play a significant role in enhancing the credibility of the diagnosis in addition to therapy choices. This paper will offer a superior approach that combines Gaussian filter and Linear Iterative Clustering Algorithm (LICA) to effectively demarcate and extract quantitative characteristics of liver tumors. The image pre-processes start with the use of Gaussian filtering on the CT images to suppress noise and preserve important anatomical information. The ROIs are manually defined based on local intensity minima to enhance focused segmentation. LICA is used to perform effective clustering according to pixel intensity and spatial closeness, and thus, accurate description of tumor extent is achieved even in complex and low-contrast regions. Following segmentation, key geometric properties (such as area) are defined using the technique, and entropy, energy, contrast and homogeneity from Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) based texture descriptors are used to quantitatively describe the shape of the tumor. The experimental results over the multiple CT image samples have demonstrated that the proposed method can achieve good segmentation accuracy and refined features for further classification. Tumor detection is consistently achieved over a wide range of tumor sizes and contrast levels with low false detection rates. In addition, its high computational efficiency allows the possibility of real-time integration in the clinical environment. Unlike deep learning models, the proposed system is fully interpretable thus making it more suitable for healthcare industry, where the need for explanation is crucial.

title: Design and Comparative Analysis of PID Controllers on FPGA for Power-Sensitive Applications Using Various Arithmetic Architectures
authors: Nowreen Mukhtar, Liyaqat Nazir, Asifa Mehraj Baba
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: This study investigates the design and implementation of Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controllers on FPGA hardware, focusing on the trade-offs between various arithmetic architectures (Ripple Carry Adders (RCA) and Carry Look-Ahead Adders (CLA)) across variable bitwidth architectures. The results demonstrate different performance characteristics by comparing area, power consumption, delay, and Area Delay Product (ADP). RCA demonstrates superior area efficiency, utilizing 2.35% fewer Look-Up Tables (LUTs) than CLA (e.g., 26942 vs. 27575 LUTs for 64-bit) and lower power consumption (1.255W vs. 1.398W for 64-bit). Conversely, CLA achieves higher operational speeds (155.424 MHz vs. 98.154 MHz for 8-bit) and reduced delays (6.434 ns vs. 10.188 ns for 8-bit). However, CLA incurs a 11.35% power penalty at 64-bit and exhibits 3.25% higher ADP at 64-bit, underscoring a critical speed-resource trade-off. These results emphasize the necessity of selecting adder architectures aligned with application priorities: RCA for power, and area-sensitive systems and CLA for latency-critical implementations. The study provides actionable insights for optimizing FPGA-based PID controllers, paving the way for future exploration of hybrid architectures to balance efficiency and performance.

title: Perceptually Weighted Late Fusion for Robust Binocular Gaze Estimation in Unconstrained Environments
authors: Ngawang Choeda, Biranchi Panda, Phub Namgay
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The variation in asymmetric illumination and artifacts in ocular regions poses a significant challenge for gaze estimation when moving from controlled laboratories to a realworld environment, despite its promising applications in multiple domains. The standard gaze estimation methods either assume a perfect ocular symmetry or use simple fusion techniques, often overlooking minor interocular asymmetries. This paper introduces a perceptually weighted late fusion framework inspired by human perception for binocular gaze estimation, which implements a SoftMax-weighted fusion of each eye's gaze estimates within a dual-stream LightGazeNet architecture, using the Weber contrast between the sclera (white) and iris (colored) regions of the eye as a perceptual quality metric. Experiments on 15,000 samples from 15 participants in the MPIIGaze dataset show that the proposed method reduces the mean Euclidean error to 60.63 mm, achieving a 6.67% and 12.53% improvement compared to “Left-Eye-Only” and “Right-Eye-Only” baseline models, in addition to reducing the error variance on both axes.

title: Digital Forensic AI You Can Explain: A Case Study on Video Source Camera Identification
authors: Maryna Veksler, Kemal Akkaya, Selcuk Uluagac
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: In recent years, artificial intelligence (AI) has significantly impacted digital forensics, yet its broader deployment remains limited due to the difficulty of explaining AI decisions. Explainable AI (XAI) presents a promising solution to increase transparency and trust, but its application in digital forensics is still underexplored. In this work, we present a practical and structured explainable digital forensics AI (xDFAI) approach tailored to the forensic task of video source camera identification (VSCI). Our method enables forensic examiners to interpret the behavior of AI models, assess whether decisions are driven by intended logic or arise from random or content-dependent artifacts, and establish the integrity and reliability of explanations. We implement and evaluate this approach on two state-of-the-art VSCI models, providing step-by-step analysis of explanation quality, spatial consistency of high-impact features, and content dependence. Our results reveal that although models achieve strong classification accuracy, their explanations lack spatial stability and are impacted by video content, raising concerns about forensic reliability. To support reproducibility and future research, we provide an open-source implementation. This work underscores the potential of XAI to improve transparency in digital forensics and highlights the challenges of interpreting and presenting results. Our study takes an important step toward the operational deployment of xDFAI in multimedia forensics.

title: Hybrid EfficientNet-SVM Model for Analysis of Health Insurance Contract Breaches: An Academic Perspective
authors: J. Gayathri, Behin R.V, M. Sandeep Kumar, D Prerna, Sri Supraja S N, Vardini Venket
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Emerging Trends in Electronic Devices and Computational Techniques (EDCT)
abstract: Health insurance contract breaches are a critical issue, often leading to legal disputes between policyholders and insurers. This study presents a novel hybrid model combining EfficientNet and Support Vector Machine (SVM) for the legal analysis of health insurance contract breaches. The model is designed to identify and classify breaches in health insurance contracts, leveraging advanced feature extraction and classification techniques. Data was sourced from public legal records, judgments, and dispute summaries, followed by preprocessing steps such as anonymization, tokenization, and vectorization using TF-IDF and Word2Vec. The hybrid EfficientNet-SVM model integrates deep learning and machine learning to extract features from text-image data and classify cases into “Valid Breach,” “Invalid Breach,” and “Ambiguous” categories. The model achieved an overall classification accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 2. 8 \%}$</tex>, outperforming standalone EfficientNet (89.5 %) and SVM (85.3 %). This demonstrates the effectiveness of the hybrid approach in capturing the complexities of legal contract analysis. The study provides a scalable and interpretable solution for legal practitioners and policymakers, aiding in dispute resolution and regulatory compliance. Future enhancements include expanding the dataset to cover additional regions and integrating the model into user-friendly tools for real-time legal analysis.

title: Eff-GRot: Efficient and Generalizable Rotation Estimation with Transformers
authors: Fanis Mathioulakis, Gorjan Radevski, Tinne Tuytelaars
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: We introduce Eff-GRot, an approach for efficient and generalizable rotation estimation from RGB images. Given a query image and a set of reference images with known orientations, our method directly predicts the object’s rotation in a single forward pass, without requiring object- or category-specific training. At the core of our framework is a transformer that performs a comparison in the latent space, jointly processing rotation-aware representations from multiple references alongside a query. This design enables a favorable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency while remaining simple, scalable, and fully end-to-end. Experimental results show that Eff-GRot offers a promising direction toward more efficient rotation estimation, particularly in latency-sensitive applications. Code is available at: https://github.com/fmathiou/eff-grot.

title: GPU Accelerated Near-Field Electromagnetic Simulations for Microwave Hyperthermia Treatment Planning Using GprMax
authors: Rahul Choudhary, Kavitha Arunachalam
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Hyperthermia (HT) treatment elevates tumor temperature to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 0}-\mathbf{4 4}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>, enhancing the effectiveness of conventional cancer therapies. Microwave antennas, or applicators, are widely used to achieve localized heating, and they require patient-specific hyperthermia treatment planning (HTP) based on electromagnetic (EM) simulations. Most reported work relies on commercial solvers, which add cost and limit accessibility. In this study, we propose a GPU accelerated opensource HTP framework using gprMax, a finite-difference timedomain (FDTD) solver originally developed for groundpenetrating radar. A cavity-backed patch antenna and a twoelement array operating at 434 MHz were simulated in a muscle equivalent digital phantom. Antenna reflection coefficient and specific absorption rate (SAR) distribution were extracted from the computed electric field distributions and compared with results from commercial solver Ansys HFSS. GPU acceleration resulted in an eight-fold decrease in simulation time relative to CPU-based computation in gprMax. The study demonstrates that gprMax can provide accurate and efficient EM simulations for microwave hyperthermia applicators, offering a cost-effective, and accessible alternative for clinical treatment planning.

title: A Novel Multi-Priority Encoder with Efficient Dynamic Reordering and Broad Input Compatibility
authors: A. Subbulakshmi, K Ebinesh, V Sankaranarayanan, D. Denceli
publish: 2026 1st International Electronics &amp; Packaging Technologies Conference: Bridging Skills &amp; Innovation for India’s Industry (EPT India)
abstract: The Priority encoders are fundamental components in high-speed digital design and facilitate effective data processing and generation of control signals. Traditional topologies of priority encoders are hindered by high latency and power consumption due to high switching activity and suboptimal logic structures. Adhering to these limitations, this work presents a new topology of a multi-bit priority encoder with new dynamic approaches to priority selection. The suggested architecture includes a strong input selection framework with smooth compatibility support for different input sizes (8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit). A leading-one detector (LOD) system is also employed to enhance priority detection, and a masking decoder is employed to eliminate the highest-priority bit, enabling progressive extraction of priorities until the entire set is processed. Implementation and simulation were carried out using Cadence Virtuoso at a 90 nm technology node. The priority encoder proposed has a very low latency of 0.272 ns, with a total power consumption of 0.6 mW. Furthermore, the design shows a Power-Delay Product (PDP) of $\mathbf{2 5 f J}$, representing very high energy efficiency in comparison to traditional methods.

title: Bio-inspired Soft Crawling Robot HISOYAKA II: Adaptive Behavior Emerging from the Dynamics of a Redesigned Soft Arm
authors: Koki Hirota, Kazuyuki Ito
publish: 2026 International Conference on Image Processing and Robotics (ICIPRoB)
abstract: We investigate a mobile robot that locomotes using two long octopus-like soft arms in environments with many protruding objects. In this study, we improve the previous robot by redesigning the arms to enable non-uniform motion. A prototype is developed, and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed arms achieve more complex and adaptive motion than conventional designs, despite employing the same simple controller. The reachable area of the arm is approximately three times larger than that of the conventional design.

title: Miniaturized Dual-Band Patch Antenna for Reliable Sensing Applications
authors: Soham Karak, Swapnaleena Biswas, Sneha Shaw, Ch. Raghavendra, A.Jhansirani, Gobinda Sen
abstract: This work presents the design and execution of a triangular, inexpensive microwave sensor for multiband sensing applications. The suggested sensor makes use of a Triangular Ring Resonator (TRR) as radiating patch, which gives a high Q factor. The design with footprint of size 9 mm × 31.32 mm make it very small and hence useful for portable applications. The sensor's substrate is chosen as a low loss Arlon material with a dielectric constant of 2.5 and a loss tangent of 0.0013. In order to characterize various oil samples, toluene, and ethanol etc., the sensor provides a strong sensing characteristic at the ISM bands with a high Q value of 21.7. The highest sensitivity of 16.75% is obtained with proposed sensor by submerging it within the sample material and hence make it very convenient for real-time monitoring in industrial, agricultural, and healthcare applications because to their affordability and small size, especially when it comes to guaranteeing food safety. The efficiency of the sensors is confirmed by experimental results, which also show that they have the potential to be widely used in a variety of sensing applications.

title: Optimization of Low-Noise Amplifier Circuits for Enhanced Sensor Signal Integrity
authors: Dayarnab Baidya, Athul Krishnan, Mitradip Bhattacharjee
abstract: A Low Noise Amplifier (LNA) acts as an important component in sensor system design for wireless sensing applications. In this direction, this paper demonstrates a novel approach to optimizing LNA design using Grey Relation Analysis (GRA), a multi-objective optimization method. This technique was used to increase gain (S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">21</inf>), and stability factor (K), and decrease input reflection coefficient (S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf>), reverse voltage gain (S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</inf>), output reflection coefficient (S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">22</inf>), and Noise Figure (NF) for the LNA circuit. The LNA circuit was simulated in the Advanced Design System (ADS) tool. The obtained optimized input values are R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> = 665 Ω, R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>= 12 Ω, L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> = 2 nH, C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> = 1.2 pF, C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</inf> = 1.2 pF. In this situation, outputs had magnitudes as S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf>= -14.007 dB, S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">22</inf>= -17.023 dB, S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</inf>= - 17.752 dB, S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">21</inf>= 11.390 dB, K = 1.210, NF= 0.882 dB at 4 GHz. Furthermore, an analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to determine the impact of inputs on LNA outputs. This study suggests that the contribution of L3 is significantly high, with 98.62%.

title: An Explainable AI-Based Hybrid SVM–CNN Ensemble Model Implementation for Intelligent Email Spam Detection
authors: Tanmoy Chanda, Biva Mondal, Subhashis Das, Soumendu Banerjee, Bappaditya Mondal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: In modern-day life, emails are mostly used as a source of communication across almost everywhere from business to education. Generally, emails can be classified into spam & ham (not spam) mail. Spam mail creates several problems in our daily life such as it wastes our time, slows down our devices, steals important information like passwords or personal details such as bank information, ATM card information, etc. Hence, the identification & removal of spam messages is a very serious & important challenge for most modern-day email systems. In that context, a hybrid ensemble model has been proposed that combines machine learning (ML) and a deep learning (DL) algorithm to detect spam mail from the recipient's email list. In this proposed technique, emails are not only classified but also provided in-depth metrics for fine-tuning the predictions, thus making it practical for real-life deployment. The proposed model is developed by combining the strengths of Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Deep Neural Network (DNN) & Convolution Neural Network (CNN) to achieve a recall value of 97.41%.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Architecture for Secure Validation of Medicines and Consumer Products
authors: K. Maithili, S V Hemanth, S.Tejas Reddy, S. Amutha, Krishnaveni N, Minu Inba Shanthini Watson Benjamin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The issue of drugs and the things that people purchase on a daily basis are a huge problem since it inflicts pain on people and ruins the reputation of businesses. It also makes the buyers not trust much. typically the process of making and selling is, however, not very transparent. People can easily counterfeit records or dispose of them. to achieve this we can apply the notion of a computer known as blockchain. This system allows all the concerned parties to confirm whether a drug or other things that people purchase are authentic or not. It operates by record keeping of all that occurred to a product since it is manufactured to when it is sold in a book which no one is allowed to alter once written. A smart contract will record all the events that occur to an item automatically. This implies that the entire life of the item since it was in the factory to the customer is never hidden. each package has its QR code attached to its data in a ledger. As a buyer scans this code with their phone they can view away whether the item is real or not on Ethereum. We have a prototype that is Ethereum based. The contracts of this prototype are in Solidity. We also have a Python module that links the phone application to the blockchain.

title: Real-Time Crop Sensing and Localization for Precision Agriculture
authors: Rakesh M D, Rohith Rajesh, Rudraswamy S B
abstract: This work introduces a real-time intelligent framework for subterranean crop sensing, depth estimation, and object tracking, designed to advance precision agriculture. Leveraging a custom-trained YOLOv11n model, the system accurately identifies crops such as beetroot, radish, and potatoes from RGB imagery and live video feeds. To determine spatial positioning, the approach integrates MiDaS a monocular depth estimation technique which infers Z-axis coordinates without the need for specialized depth sensors. The fusion of YOLO bounding box centers with MiDaS-generated depth maps enables estimation of actual crop-to-camera distances. Norfair-based multi-object tracking assigns unique IDs and preserves crop identity across sequential frames, ensuring consistency in dynamic agricultural settings. The end-to-end system delivers comprehensive spatial awareness vital for autonomous harvesting, targeted input application, and yield estimation. A user-centric Gradio interface supports intuitive interaction and visualization, making the tool accessible to both experts and farmers. Emphasizing scalability and adaptability, the framework can be easily customized for different crop types through dataset retraining. By supporting efficient resource utilization and promoting smart farming practices, this work directly contributes to key United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, including SDG 2, SDG 9 and SDG 12.

title: Explainable Multimodal Vision Transformer for CKD Prediction and Stage Classification
authors: V Nivethitha, K S Manoj, K Sai Poojitha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is a progressive disease that is mostly asymptomatic and remains in an undiagnosed state until it reaches an advanced stage of the disease, thereby increasing morbidity and mortality rates and creating a healthcare burden for patients around the world. Hence, it is of prime importance that CKD be identified at an early stage in order to stratify the risk of progression of the disease. Recent works have shown the presence of microvascular markers related to renal disorders in retinal fundus images, allowing for a non-invasive CKD screening method. In this manuscript, we present an explainable multimodal deep learning approach unifying retinal Fundus images and structured clinical data for early CKD diagnosis, staging, and classification. To do this, a Vision Transformers ViT is used for the extraction of global features from retinal images, combined with a deep neural network for the processing of clinical variables, like HbA1c levels, blood pressure, creatinine levels, and estimated glomerular filtration rate. The learned features are combined and optimized for all tasks simultaneously as part of a multi-task learning approach. To improve explainability and trustworthiness of the results, a gradient-based method for explainability is employed as part of the proposed approach, namely Grad-CAM. Experimentation carried out confirms that the proposed approach outperforms traditional CNN-based as well as unimodal schemes for CKD detection, and this establishes that this approach could potentially be used as a trustworthy tool for CKD detection as a decision-support system.

title: Boosting Unsupervised Video Instance Segmentation with Automatic Quality-Guided Self-Training
authors: Kaixuan Lu, Mehmet Onurcan Kaya, Dim P. Papadopoulos
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Video Instance Segmentation (VIS) faces significant annotation challenges due to its dual requirements of pixel-level masks and temporal consistency labels. While recent unsupervised methods like VideoCutLER eliminate optical flow dependencies through synthetic data, they remain constrained by the synthetic-to-real domain gap. We present AutoQ-VIS, a novel unsupervised framework that bridges this gap through quality-guided self-training. Our approach establishes a closed-loop system between pseudo-label generation and automatic quality assessment, enabling progressive adaptation from synthetic to real videos. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with 52.6 AP<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</inf> on YouTubeVIS-2019 val set, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art VideoCutLER by 4.4%, while requiring no human annotations. This demonstrates the viability of quality-aware self-training for unsupervised VIS. The source code of our method is available at here.

title: SymNet: A Multi-Task Network for Joint Radio Map Reconstruction and Transmitter Localization
authors: Lyuzhou Ye, Thanh Dat Le, Yan Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Accurately predicting directional radio maps is essential for wireless applications, yet prior approaches primarily focus on omnidirectional signals and typically treat transmitter localization and signal map reconstruction as separate tasks. In omnidirectional settings, predicting the maximum signal location often coincides with the transmitter position, which limits the need for explicit joint modeling. However, in directional propagation where angular effects, reflections, and building occlusions play critical roles, this assumption no longer holds. To address this gap, we propose SymNet, a unified framework that jointly predicts directional radio maps and transmitter locations from sparse signal measurements. SymNet incorporates a prediction head for transmitter localization alongside radio map reconstruction, enabling simultaneous learning of both tasks. This joint formulation leverages their complementary information and leads to consistent improvements over treating them separately. Experiments on challenging directional scenarios demonstrate that SymNet outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving superior accuracy in both radio map reconstruction and transmitter localization.

title: Key Point Iris Detection Using Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Sweta Jain, Devishree Naidu, Atharva Nagbhidkar, Atharva Pawar, Aryan Dhait
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: The proposed research demonstrates a novel iris recognition solution using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in the task of key point detection. Unlike the state-of-the-art solutions in the field, where the entire iris texture image is considered for analysis, the purpose of this solution is the effective and accurate detection of robust and distinct iris features like the iris boundary point, the pupil point, and other significant biologically based key points of interest. Additionally, the solution uses the ResNet152v2 CNN structure that proves efficient in extracting high spatial details of highresolution images. Furthermore, the CNN consists of down sampling and refinement steps that allow the gradual focusing of interest regions on the object of interest identification. This research highlights the viability of the iris recognition solution based on key point detection in biometric border control applications and other digital product identifications in the field of medicine. The Mean Squared Error for the model was 0.0027 on average, with a precision of 94.6 %, recall of 92.1 %, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> score of 93.3% for the test set.

title: AI-Powered Real-Time Vehicle Detection and Traffic Analysis using YOLOv3 and OpenCV
authors: S Niresh Kumar, A. Hemalatha, P Malathi, T.N. Charanya, K Antony Kumar, P Rajendran
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: Vehicle detection and classification is a very important task in traffic monitoring and autonomous driving and intelligent surveillance. Real-time detection and classification of various vehicle types, including cars, trucks, buses, and motorcycles, is essential. This can actually make the road safer. This can actually help in the traffic flow and the police. However, the traditional way of vehicle detection faces some problems like when one vehicle is blocking another vehicle, when the light is poor, when the city is very complicated. So, we need to develop a system. This research aims to develop a better system for vehicle detection and classification by using OpenCV and YOLOv3. The main objective of this research is to develop a model which can detect and classify different kinds of vehicles in an efficient way. The system which we are proposing can look at the video in time. Can identify different kinds of vehicles very fast and accurately in different kinds of traffic situations. This research uses OpenCV to process images and YOLOv3 to detect objects in time. YOLOv3 is very good at detecting objects. This research uses YOLOv3 to detect objects like vehicles in images. Then we classify them based on their appearances. A fine-tuned YOLOv3 model is used for vehicle detection and classification. The system is developed in Python, with OpenCV for video processing and deep learning frameworks for model implementation. Results show effective performance under various conditions. This system is effective at processing video feeds with little delay. This makes it effective for real-time applications. This research is helpful in the management of traffic because we have a way to monitor vehicles automatically. This can be used in transportation systems as well as security surveillance. In conclusion this research shows that using OpenCV and YOLOv3 together is a way to detect and classify vehicles in time.

title:
Benchmarking YOLOv12-A
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    F for Robust Detection of Underwater Objects
authors: Rajat Amat, Mourina Ghosh, Amitava Nag, Robin Pathak
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: In order to solve issues including turbidity, poor contrast, and color distortion, this study benchmarks the YOLOv12-A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>F model for reliable underwater object recognition. The model was trained and assessed on an underwater dataset encompassing varied item classifications and varying optical conditions. YOLOv12-A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>F obtained strong performance, with a precision of 85.6%, recall of 73.8%, mAP@50 of 81.5% and mAP@50-95 of 49.5%, exhibiting dependable localization and classification. With only 29.0 million parameters, the model stays efficient while delivering comparable accuracy. These findings demonstrate that YOLOv12-A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>F is a powerful and portable solution for problems involving underwater perception.

title: AuViRe: Audio-visual Speech Representation Reconstruction for Deepfake Temporal Localization
authors: Christos Koutlis, Symeon Papadopoulos
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of sophisticated synthetic audio-visual content, e.g., for subtle malicious manipulations, ensuring the integrity of digital media has become paramount. This work presents a novel approach to temporal localization of deepfakes by leveraging Audio-Visual Speech Representation Reconstruction (AuViRe). Specifically, our approach reconstructs speech representations from one modality (e.g., lip movements) based on the other (e.g., audio waveform). Cross-modal reconstruction is significantly more challenging in manipulated video segments, leading to amplified discrepancies, thereby providing robust discriminative cues for precise temporal forgery localization. AuViRe outperforms the state of the art by +8.9 AP@0.95 on LAV-DF, +9.6 AP@0.5 on AV-Deepfake1M, and +5.1 AUC on an in-the-wild experiment. Code at https://github.com/mever-team/auvire.

title: Campus Lifestyle Analytics and Academic Success Using IoT-Based Behavioural Tracking
authors: G. Swarnalatha, S. Mohan, G Saabakarini
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: With the increase in the number of IoT devices in schools, there has been an opportunity to utilize behavioral analytics to further analyze and enhance students’ academic performance. Combing lifestyle indicators in one analytics system for predicting academic success, this study presents an innovative IoT-based behavioral monitoring framework. The framework would consider variables such as attendance habits, library use, usage of study spaces, sleep patterns, and social interactions. The proposed method leverages mobile sensing, RFID-tagged campus facilities, and intelligent wearables to capture non-intrusive insights into students’ daily lives in real-time, as opposed to existing survey-based or self-reported studies. Students, instructors, and educational administrators are all served by the data that is captured when machine learning techniques are utilized to identify correlations between students’ lifestyle habits and academic performance. An improved grade point average is positively related to regular lifestyle measures such as adequate sleep, optimizing study time, and regular exercise. On intelligent campus networks, this research shows the potential of internet-of-things (IoT) powered behavioral analytics as an integrated, scalable approach toward enhancing students’ health, personal learning, and academic performance in the long term.

title: An Interpretable Ensemble Neural and Symbolic Learning Framework for Pattern Discovery Across Literary Eras
authors: S. Nandhini, Bujji Babu Velagaleti, M. Udhayakumar, S. Padmapriya, Tahmeena Fatima, R. Kanagaselvam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The paper presents LitEraNet-X; an interpretable ensemble model integrating neural and symbolic learning to find patterns in literary works in five distinct periods of English literature: Renaissance, Romantic, Victorian, Modernist, and Postmodern. The framework combines a two-path architecture, which consists of a Transformer-LSTM neural network and a logic-based symbolic inference engine, which is informed by a Literary Device Knowledge Graph (LDKG). It analyzes texts using literary-conscious preprocess, motif matching, semantic drift modeling and emotion trajectory analysis. The model classifies eras with a maximum accuracy of 91.36% in Romantic texts and has F1-scores of more than 0.84 at all periods. The most essential literary patterns and emotional lines are connected with neuro-symbolic fusion and understood through SHAP values, which helps provide obvious, scholarly-relevant information. Semantic drift tracker also shows conceptual shifts of key words (e.g., freedom, virtue) over time. The consistency of the dual interpretability is confirmed by experimental findings, which show a high level of motif alignment ($\Delta{\lt}0.05$) between neural attention and symbolic reasoning. The framework promotes a field of digital humanities, which combines statistical strength and the knowledge of the literary domain. It is designed in a way that is not only accurate and deep but also transparent- between the methods of computation and humanistic inquiry.

title: Identification of Key Genes in Prostate Cancer via RNA Sequencing and Network-Based Bioinformatics
authors: VasudevaRao Badana, Ramesh Chappa, Chalapathi Rao Tippana, JagadeeswaraRao G
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Prostate cancer (PCa) stands as the second most common cancer diagnosis among men worldwide every year. Nearly 1.5 million men have been diagnosed with prostate cancer. Gene expression microarrays have identified multiple tumor markers and therapeutic targets for prostate adenocarcinoma (PRAD). Ribonucleic acid sequencing (RNA-seq), one of the most widely used transcriptomics technologies, is helpful for tumor diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment because it may demonstrate the relationship between complex biological processes and genetic mutations. Differential analysis of RNA-seq data is necessary to identify aberrant transcriptions; efficient techniques for differential expression analysis include Limma, EdgeR, and DESeq2. The goal of this work is to identify the hub genes linked to PRAD by doing an RNA-seq analysis using the DESeq2 and Limma packages. Here, we provide a comprehensive method for identifying genes that are differentially expressed (DEGs) between PRAD tissues and healthy tissues. We have taken the gene expression data for PRAD from the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), which includes 554 samples (502 tumor, 52 normal). A total of 2635 and 4348 DEGs were identified by the Limma and DESeq2 tools, respectively. Further, these DEGs were filtered based on their nature of expression and analyzed for hub gene identification through the protein-protein interaction (PPI) network, and we filtered the hub genes using the cytohubba plugin. In this study, we identified 11 hub genes that are significant in PRAD, and the gene ontology (GO) and pathway analysis of the hub genes were also presented. This thorough investigation shows how integrative genomics can be used to decipher intricate disease pathways and identifies new directions for bettering prostate cancer patient treatment.

title: Artifact Comparison Analyzer: Evaluating Microservice Build Metrics for Performance and Efficiency Improvements
authors: Sharad Jain, Dileep Jain
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Microservice architecture has become a popular method of creating scalable, modular, and maintainable software systems that allow organizations to accelerate development processes and react fast to changes in business requirements. Nevertheless, the current studies are mostly concerned with runtime performance, energy usage, and anomaly detection, with no study done on build-time efficiency or artifact-level measures. This paper introduces the Artifact Comparison Analyzer that uses the MiSAR (MicroService Architecture Recovery) model to evaluate microservice architectures in a structured way by analyzing them as artifacts. Open-source systems that were publicly available were collected and transformed into platform-specific models (PSM) and platform-independent models (PIM) based on the concepts of Model-Driven Architecture and QVTo changes. Accuracy of a model and efficacy of a framework in reference to precision, recall and F1-score evaluation metrics were used to evaluate the accuracy of the model. The two representative applications examined through experiments, TrainTicket and MusicStore, prove that MiSAR has high accuracy (up to 99.6%), successful build processing, and better resource usage. The results of optimization show a decrease in the build time, the size of the artifact, dependency resolution time, and the consumption of CPU, which demonstrates the power of the framework to perform semi-automated recovery of architectures and performance improvement. The paper confirms that MiSAR is a viable, scalable and dependable approach to evaluate and enhance microservice-based architectures.

title: Dynamic Feature Fusion In a Semi-Supervised Scene for Cardiovascular Risk Identification
authors: Turaka Rajesh, Suthir Sriram
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Predicting heart attacks isn’t just important—it’s urgent. You need models you can actually trust. That’s where our research comes in. We’re introducing Dynamic Feature Fusion in a Semi-Supervised Scene Framework. It’s a mouthful, but the idea is simple: we use multi-view data in a smarter way to make heart attack predictions sharper and more reliable. Right off the bat, SMILE tackles two big problems. First, there’s never enough labeled medical data. Second, not all types of data matter equally. Here’s what we do: the framework uses a dynamic fusion module that learns which data sources are more trustworthy, and it pays more attention to those. On top of that, we add a semantic mapping head, which helps the model build smarter features for easier classification. And with view-specific autoencoders, SMILE digs deep to pull the best low-level features from each kind of data. Because it constantly adjusts how much it trusts each data source, SMILE pulls out the strengths of every input and tones down the noise from anything low-quality. It doesn’t stop there. With so much unlabeled data floating around, SMILE leans into semi-supervised learning and pseudo-labeling, so it keeps learning even when expert-labeled examples are hard to come by. We put SMILE to the test against real-world heart attack prediction challenges. It beats other top semi-supervised, multi-view models—higher accuracy, better F1 scores, and stronger AUC. When we dig into what’s driving those results, it’s clear: the dynamic fusion and semantic mapping are making a real impact. For clinics without endless labeled data, SMILE gives them a reliable way to predict heart attacks—and honestly, it just works better.

title: Real-Time Vision-Based Disease Detection Using Edge- Optimized Deep Learning Models
authors: Jayamala Hipparkar, Altaf Mulani, Vaibhav Godase, Swapnil Takale, Rahul Ghodake
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Real-time vision-based disease detection has gained significant attention due to its potential to support early screening and rapid clinical decision-making. However, most existing deep learning models are computationally intensive and unsuitable for deployment on edge devices with limited processing power, memory, and energy resources. This limitation restricts their practical use in point-of-care and resource-constrained environments. In this paper, we propose a real-time vision-based disease detection framework using edge-optimized deep learning models. The proposed approach leverages lightweight convolutional neural network architectures and model optimization techniques to achieve an effective balance between classification accuracy and inference efficiency. Publicly available medical image datasets are used to evaluate the framework, and performance is assessed in terms of accuracy, inference latency, frames per second (FPS), and model size. Experimental results demonstrate that optimized models significantly reduce computational overhead while maintaining competitive detection performance, making the proposed framework suitable for real-time edge-based disease screening applications.

title: Analysis of Object Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Images using YOLO Frameworks
authors: Darshan Shankar R, Niharika G, Dilip Kumar Dalei, Narayan Panigrahi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Automatic analysis and Interpretation of remote sensing images is a growing area of interest among geospatial researchers and engineers. This is quite evident by observing the unprecedented growth in geospatial data and systems last decade. There are many important tasks in remote sensing image interpretation - Scene Classification, Object Detection, Change Detections etc. The object detection task in remote sensing has wide applications in both civilian and military applications. Modern remote sensing devices have made it possible to capture high-resolution spatial-temporal images of Earth's surface. Analysis of these large and complex datasets allows us to understand and interpret complex phenomena on and around earth, but the size and complexity of these datasets limit the manual methods. Deep Learning based methods has potential to handle these large and complex datasets for post analysis. Automatic object detection techniques are now feasible due to the rise in deep learning.YOLO frameworks are the industry-standard methods of object identification in images and videos. Their application in remote sensing has been on the rise, owing to their success in other domains of image. It has evolved in several versions with varied capabilities and applications. Their applicability in remote sensing image analysis is quite evident and is being researched word-wide. The current paper also explores possible solutions and challenges of fitting these models for object detection in remote sensing images. It focuses the study and analysis to understand the architecture of these models and their implementation strategy for Optical remote sensing images. The models comprise previous important versions of YOLO, including very recent YOLO 11 models.

title: Controllable Long-term Motion Generation with Extended Joint Targets
authors: Eunjong Lee, Eunhee Kim, Sanghoon Hong, Eunho Jung, Jihoon Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Generating stable and controllable character motion in real-time is a key challenge in computer animation. Existing methods often fail to provide fine-grained control or suffer from motion degradation over long sequences, limiting their use in interactive applications. We propose COMET, an autoregressive framework that runs in real time, enabling versatile character control and robust long-horizon synthesis. Our efficient Transformer-based conditional VAE allows for precise, interactive control over arbitrary user-specified joints for tasks like goal-reaching and in-betweening from a single model. To ensure long-term temporal stability, we introduce a novel reference--guided feedback mechanism that prevents error accumulation. This mechanism also serves as a plug-and-play stylization module, enabling real-time style transfer. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that COMET robustly generates high-quality motion at real-time speeds, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art approaches in complex motion control tasks and confirming its readiness for demanding interactive applications. Video results and code are available at https://comet-proj.github.io/.

title: Secure Cyber Infrastructure Design to Protect Customer Data in Modern Digital Commerce Systems
authors: P. Brindha, E. Saranya, Ganesh Kumar R, G. Manikandan, J. David Sukeerthi Kumar, Mohit Tiwari
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Emerging Trends in Electronic Devices and Computational Techniques (EDCT)
abstract: Strong, scalable, and smart customer data protection security mechanisms are required to support the continuously increasing number of online financial transactions. This paper proposes a novel decentralized cyber defense framework that combines Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) specifically designed on the blockchain, and a baseline anomaly detection model based on autoencoders. The method proposed combines ECC for lightweight encryption, Hyperledger Fabric for distributed access policy enforcement, and deep learning for high-fidelity behavioral anomaly detection. There are significant improvements in key performance metrics through experimental validation over a simulated transaction dataset. Access control decisions are enforced by the system with a 97.4 percent success rate, and latencies for encryption-decryption are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 1. 6}$</tex> milliseconds. Baseline systems were outperformed by anomaly detection by almost 8 %, with a precision of 96.2 %. Compared to baseline systems, there was an increase in end-toend transaction throughput by 37.6 %. The results show that the system can handle high user volume scenarios securely without much impact on performance.

title: Optimization of the LEACH Protocol in Wireless Sensor Networks Using Hybrid Grey Wolf and Moth Flame Optimization Algorithms
authors: Mallepula Sai Teja Goud, Akula Revanth, Askani Harish Sagar, Saroja Kumar Rout, Nikhila Kathirisetty, S Satheesh Kumar
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The general life span of WSN and Industrial WSN or remote health monitoring WSN depends exclusively on how well it can manage the depleting batteries in resource-constrained environments. Network longevity is primarily constrained by limited energy resources. Though conventional LEACH tries to save power by clustering nodes, it is a stochastic process of selecting Cluster Heads independent of the current status of the nodes, which often results in premature energy holes and network partitioning in dense deployments. In this paper, a hybrid protocol has been proposed for overcoming this variability by integrating the predatory social hierarchy of Grey Wolf Optimization with the navigational convergence of Moth Flame Optimization. Though simple, it is an effective logic to combine global exploration capability of GWO with precise local exploitation provided by MFO. Instead of being dependent on a random probability, Cluster Heads will now be selected based on optimal energy distribution and proximity metric. The strategy of hybridization could also be justified using the results as well. Compared to traditional methods and a single optimization strategy, the suggested system improves the Packet Delivery Ratio by 22.4% and the overall network lifetime by 18.6%. This plan reduces the average energy dissipation every round by 15.3% while producing a uniform residual energy distribution.

title: Integrating Spatial Diffusion and Influence Maximization for Marine Biodiversity Monitoring
authors: Sangita Dutta, Abeer Lal Nandi, Deepmalya Acharya, Himanshu Kumar, Avik Kumar Das, Uddipan Ghosh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Marine biodiversity monitoring is increasingly constrained by limited resources, vast ecological complexity, and dynamic environmental change. Traditional approaches to survey site selection, such as random seeding, Degree centrality, or PageRank heuristics, provide computational efficiency but fail to capture ecological dynamics, spatial spillover, and temporal variation. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel framework for spatially aware influence maximization in marine biodiversity monitoring. In our model, survey stations are represented as nodes in a symmetric k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) network, with edges weighted by geodesic distance decay to simulate ecological diffusion. Species spread is modeled using the Independent Cascade (IC) process augmented with geographic decay functions, enabling realistic representation of biodiversity spillover across survey sites. We introduce a minimal-K decision rule that identifies the smallest set of seed stations needed to achieve near-maximal biodiversity coverage, thereby supporting cost-effective planning under logistical constraints. Extensive experiments on real-world reef survey datasets demonstrate that our framework consistently outperforms baseline strategies including Degree, PageRank, and Random seeding across multiple coverage metrics and temporal evaluations. The results highlight the scalability, robustness, and ecological validity of our approach, offering a practical tool for adaptive conservation planning and real-time marine biodiversity management.

title: Machine Fault Identification Using a Digital Twin-Based Augmented Vision Framework with IoT-Enabled Sensors
authors: K. Elavarasi, Z. Mohammed Javith, N. Imran, K. RajKumar
publish: 2026 1st International Electronics &amp; Packaging Technologies Conference: Bridging Skills &amp; Innovation for India’s Industry (EPT India)
abstract: In modern industries, machines serve as the backbone of production. Any unexpected downtime can lead to a complete halt in operations, resulting in significant losses in productivity and revenue. Ensuring the continuous and efficient operation of machinery is therefore a critical aspect of industrial maintenance. The proposed system introduces a Digital Twin (DT)-based Augmented Vision (AV) framework designed to support predictive maintenance by integrating IoT-enabled sensors, real-time visualization, and fault diagnostics. The system employs a DT scanner to capture and visualize the operating condition of machines, providing a color-coded output—green for normal, orange for warning, and red for critical conditions such as abnormal vibration, overcurrent, overheating, or excessive noise. The approach combines IoT-based data acquisition with DT visualization to deliver an intelligent, low-cost, and efficient solution for monitoring and maintaining industrial equipment. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed system improves diagnostic accuracy, reduces service time, and ensures enhanced operational reliability environments.

title: Intelligent Cold Storage Monitoring for Perishable Fruits
authors: Balarengadurai C, Inchara Prasad, Hitha M N, Havisha V Sarovar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: In order to maintain the quality of fresh perishable fruits after harvest, it is necessary to possess ideal and consistent cold storage environments. The latest cold storage environments are applying a combination of human management and control systems that lack the precision necessary to detect subtle environmental variations that lead to spoilage and premature ripening. This paper addresses a methodology for monitoring cold storage environment based on a specifically designed intelligent cold monitoring system consisting of a distributed sensor network and data-driven analytical models. This system permits measurement of temperature, humidity, gas accumulation, and air quality on a continuous basis, thereby providing a comprehensive account of the cold storage environment. When certain measurements are integrated with machine learning tools, they are capable of identifying outliers, forecasting outcomes and enabling participation in timely decision-making. A laboratory scale cold storage chamber evaluated the proposed system over multiple fruit batch experiments. Based on quantitative results, the proposed system demonstrates a 18-25% increase in shelf life, a nearly 30% decrease in spoilage, and a 10-12% energy savings compared to the current approach of monitoring temperature through set thresholds. Future scalability of the proposed innovative system will also allow for application in commercial/industrial refrigeration facilities and other low-cost system architectures.

title: Machine Learning Based Hybrid RF–VLC Modulation Classification with RIS-Assisted Channels
authors: M. W. P. Maduranga, Durgesh Nandan, W. C. Nirmal, H. K. I. S. Lakmal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Image Processing and Robotics (ICIPRoB)
abstract: This work targets Machine Learning based modulation classification system in a hybrid Radio Frequency (RF) and Visible Light Communication (VLC) environment. The setup was also supported with Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS). An indoor space of dimensions a 5 m × 5 m × 3 m was simulated and it contained a ceiling mounted LED, an RF transmitter and several RIS panels which help generate additional VLC paths by reflection. Five modulation formats were produced under AWGN over SNR levels ranging from 0 to 30 dB. Each sequence received was represented through time-domain statistics, selected FFT components, and I/Q-based features. A total of four ML classifiers, SVM, Random Forest, KNN and XGBoost were trained to identify the type of modulation. The classification accuracy increasing with SNR is shown by the results, with ensemble models achieving the highest accuracies. XGBoost shows an accuracy of 53% at 30 dB. RIS reflections provided a clear benefit at low SNR as it boosted received optical power, while causing minor degradation at high SNR due to small waveform distortions. With these findings, it is clear that unified RF–VLC modulation recognition is feasible and also shows the disadvantages introduced by RIS.

title: WWE-UIE: A Wavelet & White Balance Efficient Network for Underwater Image Enhancement
authors: Ching-Heng Cheng, Jen-Wei Lee, Chia-Ming Lee, Chih-Chung Hsu
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) aims to restore visibility and correct color distortions caused by wavelength-dependent absorption and scattering. Recent hybrid approaches, which couple domain priors with modern deep neural architectures, have achieved strong performance but incur high computational cost, limiting their practicality in real-time scenarios. In this work, we propose WWE-UIE, a compact and efficient enhancement network that integrates three interpretable priors. First, adaptive white balance alleviates the strong wavelength-dependent color attenuation, particularly the dominance of blue-green tones. Second, a wavelet-based enhancement block (WEB) performs multi-band decomposition, enabling the network to capture both global structures and fine textures, which are critical for underwater restoration. Third, a gradient-aware module (SGFB) leverages Sobel operators with learnable gating to explicitly preserve edge structures degraded by scattering. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that WWE-UIE achieves competitive restoration quality with substantially fewer parameters and FLOPs, enabling real-time inference on resource-limited platforms. Ablation studies and visualizations further validate the contribution of each component. The source code is available at https://github.com/chingheng0808/WWE-UIE.

title: Energy Optimization and Trust-Based Security Model for Enhanced WSN Performance
authors: Neela Kiranmai, R Raja Sekhar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) find their applications in essential fields like environmental monitoring, healthcare, and defence yet they have been having a consistent challenge concerning the restricted power resources and exposure to security threats. In order to deal with these problems, the given paper offers a dual-module integrated framework that will include the energy optimization and improvement of the trust. The former relies on LiqOG-Net (Liquid Neural Networks + Osprey Optimization + GRU) to perform adaptive clustering and efficient selection of cluster head to minimize the energy loss and prolong the lifetime of the network. The second module relies on LiteSBLA-Net ( SqueezeNet + Bi-LSTM + Adam optimizer ) to develop a powerful trust management and anomaly detector system that can detect and counteract attacks, including Blackhole, Sybil, Selective Forwarding, Replay, Jamming, and Sinkhole. The experimental findings indicate vast improvements in the experimental period as the network life cycle is achieved by a significant percentage, the average energy usage is minimized, and more than 98 percent of the detection of malicious nodes is achieved, which is testament to the fact that the proposed framework will provide sustainable, secure, and high-performance WSNs operations.

title: Analysis of Dual AC-DC Charging for Electric Vehicles using Fuzzy Logic Controller
authors: M. Yerri Veeresh, M S Shiva Kumar, Y V Siva Reddy, V.Nagabhaskar Reddy, M.Vasu, U V Sujala, K.Rajasekhara Reddy, K. Harish
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Recent breakthroughs in electric vehicle (EV) driving range, battery power density, and charger ratings, a number of difficulties remain that prevent mainstream EV adoption and parity with internal combustion engine vehicles. The key challenges are inadequate charging infrastructure, long charging times, potential grid overload and congestion, non-uniform EV charging standards, and costly capital expenditures. EVs are typically charged using a single charging input, whether AC or DC. However, in residential areas and regions without rapid charging stations, DC charging outlets are frequently underutilized, forcing EV users to rely on slower AC charging choices. Rooftop solar photovoltaic (PV) systems, battery energy storage systems (BESS), and vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) charging are emerging options that offer alternate DC charging channels. This paper presents a dual charging technique that uses a solar-PV array and a BESS to supplement the power provided by standard on-board AC chargers (OBC), allowing an EV battery to be charged simultaneously through both its AC and DC ports. The proposed methodology using FLC is simulated and studied in MATLAB/Simulink to demonstrate its feasibility and efficiency in lowering charging time while optimizing the use of existing distributed energy resources.

title: SeqFeedNet: Sequential Feature Feedback Network for Background Subtraction
authors: Yu-Shun Huang, Jing-Ming Guo, Yi-Xiang Yang
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Background subtraction (BGS) is a fundamental task in computer vision with applications in video surveillance, object tracking, and recognition. Despite recent advancements, many deep learning-based BGS algorithms rely on large models to extract high-level representations, demanding significant computational resources and leading to inefficiencies in processing video streams. To address these limitations, we introduce the Sequential Feature Feedback Network (SeqFeedNet), a novel supervised algorithm for BGS in unseen videos that operates without additional preprocessing models. SeqFeedNet innovatively incorporates time-scale diverse sequential features and employs a feedback mechanism for each iteration. Moreover, we propose the Sequential Fit Training (SeqFiT) technique, enhancing model convergence during training. Evaluated on the CDNet 2014 dataset, SeqFeedNet not only achieves ∼ 5 times increase in inference speed but also outperforms F-Measure scores of the leading supervised algorithms, making it highly suitable for real-world applications. Our experiment demonstrates that SeqFeedNet surpasses state-of-the-art network without pre-trained segmentation model by 3.83% F-Measure on the CDnet 2014 dataset. Leading the way to establish a new benchmark for efficient and effective BGS in unseen videos. The code is released at https://github.com/tw-yshuang/SeqFeedNet.

title: Deep Learning-Based Detection and Classification of Mango Leaf Disease Using Image Processing Techniques
authors: K. Sai Lokesh, S. Shanthi
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Mango production is important for the agricultural economy while foliar diseases such as sooty mold, powdery mildew, bacterial canker, and anthracnose adversely affect the yield and quality of mango. The early and accurate identification of mango foliar diseases is necessary for management and to minimize crop losses. In this study, we utilize convolutional neural network architectures in concert with sophisticated image processing to propose a deep learning-based methodology for the automatic identification and classification of mango leaf diseases. Preprocessing image processing techniques, photo quality improvements, image noise removal, establishing image consistency, and data augmentation, were performed systematically to enhance the diversity of elicited feature sets from images of healthy and diseased mango leaves. Four different evaluation measures accuracy, F1 score, precision, and recall were used to assess the performance of three deep learning methods applied to the images, like CNN, DenseNet, and ResNet. From the results of our tests, we can confidently say that CNN will outperform with an accuracy of 99%, DenseNet also achieves the best accuracy of 98%, and ResNet gets low accuracy of 97%. This study establishes a strong basis for the early detection of diseases in mango crops and opens possibilities for the better usage of deep learning.

title: Adaptive Gait Assistance for Foot Drop Rehabilitation Based on Uncertainty Fusion of Contralateral Limb Information
authors: Kehan Xu, Jun Huo, Yize Zheng, Zixin Chi, Yu Cao, Zhaohui Yang, Jian Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Foot drop resulting from neurological injury severely compromises mobility and gait stability, yet existing assistive solutions often overlook physiological bilateral coordination and lack adaptability to individual gait variability. This study introduces a novel adaptive gait assistance framework for a soft exosuit that simultaneously aims to restore inter-limb coordination and generate personalized ankle joint assistance. First, we design a contralateral-guided adaptive phase synchronization engine that transforms healthy-limb information into an ideal phase reference for the impaired limb, enabling active correction of gait asymmetry. Second, to reconcile stability and personalization, we propose an uncertainty fusion-based adaptive gait assistance that estimates the real-time confidence of each model, arbitrates and fuses their predictions, and produces assistance trajectories that are both robust and individualized. After demonstrating high predictive accuracy and robustness in validation experiments with healthy subjects, a clinical evaluation involving five individuals with foot drop showed significant improvements in ankle kinematics, walking speed, and gait symmetry. Subjective feedback confirmed superior comfort, balance, and confidence over baseline and preset assistance. These preliminary results suggest that the proposed framework can improve gait kinematics, symmetry, and user-perceived walking performance, and support its potential as a personalized assistance strategy for foot-drop rehabilitation.

title: Metrology-Driven Comparison of Biomechanical Model of Fingers and Thumb: Evaluating Marker Configurations for Kinematic Measurements_supp1-3687323.docx
authors: Juri Taborri
abstract: Accurate recording of hand joint kinematics is crucial for fields such as clinical diagnostics, ergonomics, and sign language recognition. It relies on the precise placement of markers and the number of markers used to capture joint movements effectively. This study presents a simplified marker-based biomechanical model of the hand designed to compute 22 joint angles using only 24 passive retroreflective markers, following a one-marker-per-segment approach. Kinematic data was collected from ten participants performing predefined physiological movements. The proposed biomechanical model was compared with three established biomechanical models employing one, two, and three-markers-per-segment on the fingers. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this study is the first to directly compare these marker configurations simultaneously under identical experimental conditions. Range of motion (ROM) and joint variability were analyzed using repeated-measures ANOVA, while temporal trajectory differences were assessed via one-dimensional Statistical Parametric Mapping (SPM). Comparative analysis revealed no significant differences in ROM, variability, and joint angle trajectories across models, offering a robust and efficient approach for hand motion analysis suitable for functional, ergonomic, and AI-driven applications.

title: Reactive Current Profiles of Grid-Forming Converter and Power Plant During Voltage Sags
authors: Fangzhou Zhao, Tianhua Zhu, Yifei Li, Xiongfei Wang
abstract: As an internal voltage source, a grid-forming (GFM) converter is required to deliver a natural response of reactive current to grid voltage variations. However, this behavior differs from the current injection rule in existing grid codes, which were initially defined for grid-following converter. This article first introduces an analytical model quantifying the reactive current profile (RCP) of a GFM converter, defined as the relationship between the reactive current magnitude and voltage deviation. The model highlights the impacts of effective resistance, reactance and QV droop gain on RCP, offering clear insights into its connection and distinction from the k factor specified in existing grid codes. Then, an aggregation approach based on Thévenin equivalence is presented to describe the RCP of a GFM power plant consisting of multiple GFM converter strings. The approach provides a simple and efficient tool to evaluate the RCP at the plant level, considering the control effects and disparities within the power plant. The experiments of a downscaled converter and simulations of a nine-converter GFM power plant are presented for validation.

title: Impact of Vehicular Processing Capacity, Mobility Dynamics and Cooperativeness on R2V Task Offloading Performance In Vehicular Fog Networks
authors: Maurice Khabbaz, Hussein Hijazi, Ryan Atallah, Chadi Assi
abstract: In Vehicular Fog Computing (VFC), the per-vehicle processing capacity and mobility dynamics exert climacteric impacts on the task offloading performance of in-proximity RoadSide Units (RSUs) in terms of critical Quality-of-Service (QoS) metrics (e.g., task admission and Head-of-Line (HoL) blocking probabilities as well as the task expiry probability and mean buffering delay). Precisely, RSU buffer exhaustion, RSUto- Vehicle (R2V) denial of task release and, hence, resulting increases in the average system response time are undesirable but often inevitable whenever incoming vehicles into the RSU's range suffer from limited task processing resources and/or happen to be navigating at high speeds; hence, limiting their capability to successfully process and complete tasks within their relatively short residency within the RSU's coverage range. Existing work have already highlighted the possible occurrence of the abovementioned R2V Task Offloading (R2VTO) shortcomings. However, very few publications have quite marginally quantified the impact of vehicle processing resource availabilities and mobility dynamics on the R2VTO QoS performance. Also, the vehicles' non-cooperativeness has been largely overlooked. This work aims at closing these gaps through the establishment of a theoretical stochastic framework, throughout which the task release feasibility is derived from first principles (as opposed to being exogenously pre-defined in prior work) and the above-mentioned QoS metrics are expressed as a function of the vehicles's processing capacity, mobility characteristics and cooperativeness probability. Extensive simulations are then conducted to verify the correctness, validity and accuracy of the derived expressions and evaluate the R2VTO's performance as a function of each of the considered metrics as well as existing scheduling disciplines.

title: A Bounded-Update Commitment Protocol (BUCP) for Safe Edge–Cloud Adaptive Control of Networked Industrial Robots
authors: Yong Lin, Yadi Lin
abstract: Edge–cloud robotic systems increasingly rely on cloud-side learning or optimization to update controller parameters online. However, directly committing such updates to real-time industrial manipulators poses severe safety risks: network jitter, packet loss, and time-varying uncertainties can convert an apparently beneficial update into a destabilizing transient. This paper proposes a Bounded-Update Commitment Protocol (BUCP), a safety-gated mechanism for cloud-to-edge parameter commitment. BUCP introduces an edge-resident Safety Gateway that admits a cloud-suggested parameter vector only if it satisfies a verifiable three-layer contract: excitation sufficiency, bounded parameter jump, and predicted performance improvement. The tracking controller is instantiated as an inner-loop Slotine–Li adaptive law, and BUCP further employs a short validation window with automatic rollback to contain post-commit degradation. Under the protocol constraints, the closed-loop signals remain Uniformly Ultimately Bounded (UUB), and the update process is Zeno-free via an enforced minimum dwell time. Experiments on a SIASUN SR50A 6-DOF industrial manipulator deployed with ROS2 under diverse emulated network conditions corroborate the proposed design (servo cycle Tc = 2 ms, 500 Hz). Relative to a 10 Hz periodic update baseline, BUCP reduces the downlink commitment rate by approximately 88% (from 10 Hz to ≈1.0–1.2 Hz in our tests) while preserving near-periodic tracking accuracy in S1 and maintaining bounded degradation under harsher links, demonstrating its value as an auditable safety layer for industrial edge–cloud adaptation. Beyond conventional performance metrics, we report process-level safety indicators—unsafe-update rejection rate, rollback frequency, and recovery time—demonstrating BUCP as a pluggable and auditable safety layer for industrial edge–cloud adaptation.

title: Physics-Guided Deep Neural Networks for Radar-Based UAV Recognition in Different Environments With No Prior in Situ Data
authors: Sean Kearney, Sevgi Z. Gurbuz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
