Experimental evaluations on benchmark datasets such as UIEB and EUPV demonstrate that our approach provides considerable improvements over conventional methods in both qualitative and quantitative metrics.

title: Vidyasaarthi: A smart conversational Agent for Real-Time Support
authors: P Mounika, Samandhala Pravalika, Majoju Nandhini, Bindu Sabavath
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of "VidyaSaarthi: A Smart Conversational Agent for Real-Time Support", inspired by intelligent automation concepts used in modern IoT-based systems like the Smart Bus Ticketing model. VidyaSaarthi integrates Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to provide students, faculty, and visitors with instant, accurate, and interactive access to college-related information. The system leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), ChromaDB vector storage, and transformer models such as SBERT and Gemini for efficient data retrieval and context-aware responses. Similar to IoT-driven smart systems that automate public utilities, VidyaSaarthi automates institutional query handling to reduce administrative workload, enhance information accessibility, and improve user satisfaction. With a responsive web interface supporting both text and voice interaction, the chatbot ensures seamless communication between users and the college database. The proposed model thus offers a scalable, efficient, and intelligent solution that aligns with the digital transformation goals of educational institutions.

title: Multilingual Hope Speech Classification Framework for English, Thunglish, and Manglish in Social Media Texts
authors: Srinivasan R, Tamil Selvan V P, Vignesh S, Nandha Kumar, Nikkath Bushra S
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: This article presents a cohesive multilingual Hope Speech Classification framework that adeptly addresses linguistic variation among English, Thunglish (Tamil-English code-mixed), and Manglish (Malayalam + English) social media texts. For Thunglish and Manglish, a hybrid deep learning architecture combining XLM-RoBERTa was utilized to capture sequential and cross-lingual contextual dependencies, attaining validation accuracies of 0.678 and 0.877, respectively. The English model used a Cross-Model Attention and Neural fusion layer that combined multi-transformer encoders, MultiPooling aggregation, and MultiSample-Dropout regularization to get an accuracy of 0.94. The proposed framework shows that combining transformer-based multilingual embeddings with sequence modeling and Handcrafted feature-level fusion makes the model much more robust and able to work with low-resource and morphologically rich languages. The system sets a strong cross-lingual standard for natural language processing tasks that are based on empathy and sentiment

title: Enhancing Communication for Deaf and Hard Hearing People Using Real Time Augmented Reality Captioning Glasses with Sign Language
authors: A.R Kavitha, Avinash Venkatraman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The communication barrier remains a major challenge for DHH individuals, especially in noisy and multi-speaker scenarios, where conventional captioning or inter-pretter services may not be readily available or may suffer from considerable delay. This paper proposes a real-time augmented reality captioning glasses system with four-channel adaptive beamforming, cloud-based ASR, sign language recognition, and speaker attribution using gaze tracking. The system incorporates Google Cloud ASR as the primary ASR engine and Whisper-Tiny as the offline ASR engine. The real-time caption is superimposed within the user’s field of view using Open XR, thus providing natural eye contact and situational awareness. The experimental results show that the proposed system achieves a word error rate of 6.1% ± 0.8% in quiet environments and 9.3% ± 1.4% in noisy environments, with a mean end-to-end latency of 362 ± 25 ms under stable network connectivity. The sign recognition module achieves an accuracy of 96.2% with 5-fold cross-validation and 91.4% cross-signer generalization. The result of the usability study based on the SUS questionnaire is 82.4 ± 4.3, which is a very high usability score and reflects a very high level of user satisfaction with the proposed system. The proposed multimodal framework can provide effective support for DHH individuals in real-time communication situations.

title: Shape-Constrained Segmentation of Anatomical Structures Using Graph-Based Deep Learning in Low-Contrast CT Images
authors: Rajib Guha Thakurta, Anitha Christy Angelin. P, Mukesh Soni, Durgesh Nandan, Rajit Nair, M. W. P. Maduranga
publish: 2026 International Conference on Image Processing and Robotics (ICIPRoB)
abstract: Weak boundaries, intensity ambiguity, and structural noise make accurate segmentation of anatomical structures in low-contrast CT images challenging. This paper describes a shape-prior-constrained, graph-based deep learning framework that utilizes a unique combination of convolutional feature extraction, graph neural network (GNN) relational reasoning, and spectral graph filtering with statistical shape anatomical templates. This framework is proposed to tackle a variety of segmentation conditions and achieve anatomically-prior-consistent segmentation. We consider CT volumes as weighted graphs, which allows us to perform feature propagation by neighborhood and model long-range dependencies. We encapsulate shape priors from eigen-shape representation in energy-based optimization to promote the anatomical plausibility of a segmentation while preserving variability across different patients. Anisotropic spectral filtering on the graph Laplacian is used to enhance the coherence of boundaries and to attenuate high-frequency noise, especially in low-contrast areas. With the introduction of viable alternatives to U-Net, Attention U-Net, nnU-Net, Swin-UNet, and GNN-only models, the proposed method, through thorough and extensive experimentation, has continued to show consistent success. Achieving a Dice similarity coefficient of 0.825, Jaccard index of 0.702, and boundary F1-score of 0.768, the method outperformed the previous best baseline (nnU-Net) by about 0.8-1.4% on most of the metrics. The average symmetric surface distance (ASSD) and 95% Hausdorff distance (HD) also reflect surface accuracy, which was previously (and consistently) underreported, at 1.62 mm and 8.9 mm respectively. Its robustness is demonstrated through a volumetric overlap error of 0.298 with corroborating true positive and true negative rate values of 0.851 and 0.932 respectively. A sensitivity score of 0.851 reinforces the explanation of this method showing the highest clinical acceptability score of 3.9 The attributed boundary realism, shape plausibility, and radiologist confidence, reflect increasing clinical acceptability attributed to even limited soft signage. These results confirm the highly clinically relavent segmentation combined the explicit shape constraints and graph based learning for low-contrast CT imaging.

title: GrowTAS: Progressive Expansion from Small to Large Subnets for Efficient ViT Architecture Search
authors: Hyunju Lee, Youngmin Oh, Jeimin Jeon, Donghyeon Baek, Bumsub Ham
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Transformer architecture search (TAS) aims to automatically discover efficient vision transformers (ViTs), reducing the need for manual design. Existing TAS methods typically train an over-parameterized network (i.e., a supernet) that encompasses all candidate architectures (i.e., subnets). However, subnets partially share weights within the supernet, which leads to interference that degrades the smaller subnets severely. We have found that well-trained small subnets can serve as a good foundation for training larger ones. Motivated by this, we propose a progressive training framework, dubbed GrowTAS, that begins with training small subnets and incorporates larger ones gradually. This enables reducing the interference and stabilizing training. We also introduce GrowTAS+ that fine-tunes a subset of weights only to further enhance the performance of large subnets. Extensive experiments on ImageNet and several transfer learning benchmarks, including CIFAR-10/100, Flowers, CARS, and INAT-19, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach over current TAS methods.

title: EV Headlight Automation: Adaptive Switching and Brightness Control
authors: Jeetender Vemula, Pitla Rathnakar Sai, Munnur Charan Kumar, Lakavath Lokeshwari, Maharaju Shiva
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Our paper introduces a low-cost, headlight automation system for electric vehicles designed to lower unwanted battery drain while improving driver comfort and safety. Ambient light is detected using an LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) and decisions regarding switching and dimming are made using an Arduino UNO. A MOSFET driver manages the headlight load so that the microcontroller can control higher currents safely. The design accommodates two modes: an Automatic Mode where headlights adjust to surrounding light and turn off automatically one minute after the engine has been turned off, and a Manual Mode keeping headlights on after engine-off for short-term requirements like roadside repairs We implemented the idea in both simulation and a hardware prototype and tested it across varied lighting scenarios. Results show fast response (PWM changes in <100 ms), stable timer performance (60s±0.2s), and a clear reduction in consumed power when dimming is applied (dimmed mode lowered power by roughly 30–60% compared to full brightness). The prototype is compact, simple to integrate into current EV electrical systems, and scalable to various headlight types. In general, the system provides an easy conventional route to brighter, more energy-saving car lighting.

title: Deep Learning-Based Depression Detection: Insights from Twitter data through Hashtag Text Analysis
authors: R Usha, N. Balakrishna, S Jahnavi, P Sai Kalyan, K Bhavitha Reddy, K Madhivadhani
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Depression has emerged as a significant worldwide issue for mental well-being, and innovative strategies are required for early detection and intervention. To address this critical challenge, this project seeks to utilize the vast amounts of Twitter data by developing a robust deep learning model aimed at identifying the initial signs of depression. By training on a dataset specifically focused on depression, The suggested system is painstakingly made to identify the minute differences in evidence of a mental health condition extracted from individuals’ online posts. A key innovation of this research lies in the development of tailored word embeddings tailored for a depression-focused corpus, improving the model's capacity to identify subtle linguistic patterns that may indicate a tendency toward depression. Project also integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a Message level Sentiment Analysis framework to bolster feature extraction and enhance model performance. Experimental findings indicate that CNN–BILSTM with Attention outperforms other models in nearly all evaluation measures for the several classifier architectures under consideration include accuracy, recall, precision, and F1 macro scores. This innovative approach underscores the potential of synergistically employing advanced NLP techniques, including sentiment analysis and attention mechanisms, for more accurate and rapid mental health diagnostics. As a result, this work marks a substantial breakthrough in automated depression identification using deep learning and paves the way for future enhancements in deep learning to support mental health research and potential interventions.

title: Parallel Hybrid Classical-Quantum Architecture for AI Safety and LLM Jailbreak Detection
authors: Sajal Bajaj, Kamlesh Dutta
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly vulnerable to adversarial "jailbreak" attacks designed to elude safety and privacy controls. Detection is still a challenging task due to the complexity of adversarial prompts and labeled data scarcity. While fine-tuning a traditional Transformer model, such as BERT and its variants, provides strong backbone results with frequently limited recall of complex edge cases, it often comes with extensive computational costs. This work proposes a parallel hybrid classical-quantum architecture. Unlike previous serial approaches which constraint information through a quantum circuit, the proposed model uses an ensemble of a classical multilayer perceptron and a data re-uploading quantum circuit, working in parallel on top of a partially unfrozen BERT backbone. Results on the Jailbreak Classification task show that this indeed provides promising results by serving as a residual correction mechanism. The proposed parallel model achieves an F1-score of 0.911 and an accuracy of 0.910, outperforming the fully fine-tuned classical BERT baseline by over 3%. Apart from the hybrid model, several Serial Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) arrangements have been considered. These lightweight quantum-only classifiers achieve results comparable to the classical baseline across various metrics by only using a few thousand trainable parameters and operating on heavily compressed BERT embeddings. What is more important is that this emphasizes the fact that compact quantum circuits can outline meaningful decision boundaries under extreme dimensionality constraints. Therefore, such quantum components can reinforce the efficiency and scalability perspective as modules for the safety-focused monitoring of LLMs systems.

title: Mapping Marine Biodiversity Vulnerability for Offshore Wind Development: Insights from Pantelleria Island
authors: Giovanna Cilluffo, Gianluca Sottile, Laura Ciriminna, Geraldina Signa, Agostino Tomasello, Salvatrice Vizzini
abstract: Offshore wind energy is rapidly emerging as a cornerstone of the global transition to renewable power. Yet, its deployment in marine environments demands careful ecological scrutiny to avoid unintended biodiversity impacts. This study presents a spatially explicit vulnerability assessment to support offshore wind farm (OWF) planning around Pantelleria Island, a pilot site in the central Mediterranean. We selected seven marine species of high conservation concern, based on the Marine Strategy Framework Directive (2008/56/EC), and compiled occurrence records from the Global Biodiversity Information Facility. Using nine environmental predictors, we applied four Ecological Niche Modeling algorithms—Generalized Linear Models, Random Forest, MaxEnt, and Support Vector Machines—to generate habitat suitability maps. These were integrated into a composite vulnerability map to highlight ecologically sensitive areas. Model performance was robust (AUC: 0.86–0.99), and the resulting maps offer actionable insights for minimizing biodiversity risks in OWF siting. Our approach demonstrates the value of predictive modeling in reconciling renewable energy development with marine conservation goals.

title: Hybrid Fake News Detection Using Lightweight BERT Embeddings and Random Forests with Context-Aware Similarity Retrieval
authors: Priti K. Zanje, Amit Pimpalkar, Saugat KC, Harika Vanam, Rohan Lagarwar, Roshan Raj KC
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Misinformation spread online has critical consequences regarding informed decision making as well as trust in society. We introduce a unified verification scheme that incorporates both small-scale transformer representations and the ensemble classification and contextual validation. Our procedures materialize a corpus of 28 million processed news reports, transforming all of them into 384-dimensional semantic vectors using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 Sentence Transformer architecture. Classification is based on the ensemble of 200 decision trees in the form of a Random Forests trained with 44898 balanced labeled samples. According to the system, 95% of the overall accuracy, 94% precision and 96 per cent recall were obtained in fabricated content identification and 95% precision and 93% recall in recognition of authentic news. A distinguishing innovation is shown by post-classification retrieval of semantically related articles with known sources using the GNews API that gives the user parallel evidence when cross-validating the classifiers with a lower confidence below 60%. The web application, written in Flask and Bootstrap provides intuitive prediction signs, numeric indicators of confidence and clear warnings of uncertain identification. Our solution, which incorporates effective transformer embeddings, robust ensemble-based methods and real-time contextual verification, is a viable, scalable solution to misinformation and will contribute to developing critical media consumption behavior.

title: Microscopy Images Supported Blood Infection Detection using Deep Transfer Learning: A Study
authors: S. Divakar, S. Chidharth, V. Vijayan, K. Vijayakumar, Audithan Sivaraman, Dayashankar Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: Blood-cell infection is caused due to various infectious diseases spread by the parasites and its infection level needs to be identified early and tremens is also executed to reduce its impact. The common diagnosis involves in blood sample collection and microscopy based analysis of blood-cell image (BCI) to identify the disease severity. Appropriate examination of the BCI is crucial for the analysis and this work considered a deep-learning (DL) tool for the examination. To improve the detection accuracy, this work applied image preprocessing based on CLAHE for image enhancement. Various phases in DL-tool includes the following; data collection and enhancement, deep-features extraction and implementing softmax classification, identification of best DL-model and generation of fused-features (FF), and classification and 3-fold cross validation (TCV). This research presents the experimental investigation with FF along with softmax achieved better accuracy to confirm the merit of the performance. This work provided accuracy up to $100 \%$ detection accuracy when infected and normal images are evaluated.

title: DFF-AUNet: Attention-Enhanced Framework for Historical Map Text Segmentation
authors: Rohan Barman, Arnab Dey, Samit Biswas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Historical maps are invaluable cultural artifacts that preserve geographic and societal information from the past. Nevertheless, these maps are so densely filled with the content of the map, have irregular typography, and are physically deteriorated that automated text segmentation becomes very challenging. Accurate segmentation is essential for extracting place names and annotations that support digital preservation and semantic map analysis. Conventional image processing and standard segmentation models can hardly cope with complex textures and the faded text that are usually found in historical maps. This study presents Dynamic Feature Fusion Attention U-Net (DFF-AUNet), a novel deep learning architecture for efficient and robust text segmentation in historical maps. The DFF-AUNet, an extension of the conventional U-Net, introduces a novel Dynamic Feature Fusion (DFF) mechanism for adaptive integration of multi-scale features, applies depthwiseseparable convolutions for reducing computations, and integrates lightweight coordinate and channel-spatial attention modules to enhance context understanding and localize the text accurately on a complex cartographical background. On the David Rumsey Historical Map dataset, the experiments show that DFF-AUNet outperforms U-Net, U-Net++, Squeeze UNet, DeepLabV3+, and SegFormer in terms of segmentation accuracy, Dice coefficient, and IoU, and also keeps high efficiency. The proposed framework offers a scalable solution for historical map text segmentation, thereby advancing comprehensive historical map understanding and digital cartographic analysis.

title: A Solar-Powered IoT-Based Outdoor Air Quality Monitoring System with Dual Connectivity and Data Quality Validation
authors: B. H. Sudantha, M. A. L. S. K. Manchanayaka, C. Premachandra, Chan-Yun Yang, D. M. U. Nethmina, W. M. P. G. B. C. Bandara
publish: 2026 International Conference on Image Processing and Robotics (ICIPRoB)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a solar-powered, IoT-enabled outdoor air quality monitoring system capable of real-time measurement of key air pollutants and environmental parameters. The system integrates an ESP32 microcontroller with multiple sensors to monitor PM2.5, PM10, CO, CO2, temperature, humidity, and pressure. Dual communication using Wi-Fi and GSM ensures reliable operation in both urban and remote environments [1]. Field deployment results demonstrate stable operation, accurate measurements, and effective AQI computation.

title: A CNN-based Transparent AI System for Early Detection of Vitiligo Through Explainability Methods
authors: T Sanjay, R Vijay Shyam, N Vinay, V. Gokula Krishnan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: Vitiligo is a chronic skin disorder which occurs by the progressive loss of melanocytes in the skin, resulting in depigmented skin patches. These patches can be very hard to find when they first start to appear. Vitiligo can be hard to find initially because it often presents as small, subtle and is often neglected. To address these limitations, this paper presents an explainable artificial intelligence (XAI)–based vitiligo detection framework that combines high diagnostic accuracy with clinical transparency. The proposed system detects vitiligo by analyzing and comparing skin images. It utilizes a hybrid deep learning architecture called EfficientNet–Vision Transformer (EfficientNet–ViT), which combines the feature extraction capability of EfficientNet with the attention mechanism of the Vision Transformer to look at skin pictures and regular medical images. In the first step it makes sure all the images are of same size and color. Then it removes unwanted portion of the image which is not required for the analysis and then it add image to the group to make the system work by comparing different kinds of similar processed image. This system can find differences in the color and texture of the skin along with the shape of any spots on the skin. The proposed system performs this to decide if something is wrong or not. The Convolutional Neural Network is really good at finding these differences because it looks at the image in a special way. It is difficult to understand the decisions made by the deep learning model. To address this issue, we apply explanation techniques like GradCam and Shape analyze and visualize the predictions which makes it easy to understand. These help to find which parts of the image are important for the model to make predictions. After testing this model with different sets of images, the final results obtained are very good with the model being about (96%) accurate, (96.4%) precise, (97.3%) recall score, (96.8%) f1-score compared to existing methodology.

title: Spec-Gloss Surfels and Normal-Diffuse Priors for Relightable Glossy Objects
authors: Georgios Kouros, Minye Wu, Tinne Tuytelaars
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Accurate reconstruction and relighting of glossy objects remains a longstanding challenge, as object shape, material properties, and illumination are inherently difficult to disentangle. Existing neural rendering approaches often rely on simplified BRDF models or parameterizations that couple diffuse and specular components, which restrict faithful material recovery and limit relighting fidelity. We propose a relightable framework that integrates a microfacet BRDF with the specular-glossiness parameterization into 2D Gaussian Splatting with deferred shading. This formulation enables more physically consistent material decomposition, while diffusion-based priors for surface normals and diffuse color guide early-stage optimization and mitigate ambiguity. A coarse-to-fine environment map optimization accelerates convergence, and negative-only environment map clipping preserves high-dynamic-range specular reflections. Extensive experiments on complex, glossy scenes demonstrate that our method achieves high-quality geometry and material reconstruction, delivering substantially more realistic and consistent relighting under novel illumination compared to existing Gaussian splatting methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/gkouros/SpecGloss-GS.

title: IOT Enabled Water Quality Surveillance System
authors: M Anjeeswari, K Ramya, D Shalini, M Bharathi, M Indumathi, M Nithya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The rapid growth of cities and industries indicates it’s essential to keep focus on water quality to preserve both human health and aquatic ecosystems. The traditional monitoring processes involves manual sampling and transport to the laboratory, leading to delayed notification, high operating costs, poor temporal resolution in time; additionally, it is not a means for detecting short-term contamination events with necessarily rapid response. Existing approaches also have scalability limitations, static sampling and delayed feedback because of cloud only computation. The proposed IoT-based water quality monitoring system with multiparameter sensors operating for pH, turbidity along with total dissolved solids and temperature together integrated using edge analytics and cloud-based analysis. The proposed system features real-time sampling of interfering sources, short latency, and low power-consumption basis on a local filtering, automatic validation, adaptive sampling scheme and situational-aware data communication. With the laboratory samples test, pH and temperature accuracy are 97.6% and 99.0%, TDS accuracy is 98.7%, an alert latency is around 3 s, over six thousand pieces of sample data can be smoothly logged in less than two-hour test duration indicating better testing performance, responsiveness and scalability as compared to traditional solutions.

title: Incipient Location and Fault Detection in Underground Cable Through Traveling Waves and Integrated AI Systems Using Enhanced CNN and LSTM Model
authors: V Puvikko Chezhian, K Uma Maheswari, Dr Ajai Venkatesh, R Divya Dharsan, Kc Guruprasath, S Saran
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: Identifying early-stage cable defects aims to pinpoint local faults by analyzing high-frequency signals, detecting remote faults and specifying their exact locations along underground cables for more efficient maintenance operations. The paper proposes a method for fault detection to the first stage and the identification of the location of those faults in underground cables by combining signal processing with deep learning techniques. it obtains the voltage and current transients from the distribution cables and applies Continuous and Discrete Wavelet Transforms (DWT) to fetch the features of very high resolution which are the early fault behavior. Multi-resolution analysis helps to separate the traveling waves thus the estimation of the fault location becomes more accurate. A Clarke transformation is used to reduce the interference, and adaptive thresholds are used to distinguish faults from normal load variations. The pre-processed time windows are then input to a hybrid Convocational Neural Network (CNN)- Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) model, where CNN layers learn spatial-temporal signatures and LSTM layers track fault progression.

title: SWIMVR: Simulating Water Interaction Using Multimodal Vibro-Thermal Rendering
authors: Premankur Banerjee, Jiaxuan Wang, Olivia Means, Heather Culbertson
abstract: Reproducing realistic fluid interactions in Virtual Reality (VR) remains an open challenge due to their dynamic and multimodal nature. In this paper, we introduce SWIMVR, a wearable vibro-thermal haptic glove that simulates handwater interactions in VR through coordinated cold thermal and vibrotactile feedback. SWIMVR delivers event-driven, spatiotemporally modulated actuation to render both surface impact and submerged flow sensations. Built around 14 micro-thermoelectric cooling modules and 13 vibrotactile actuators per hand, the system operates in real-time with a VR ocean environment rendered in Unity. We conducted two user studies optimizing thermal waveform patterns for perceptual stability and comfort, and actuator layouts for perceptual fidelity with minimal hardware. Results show that triangular thermal modulation, achieved by cycling between two cooling levels rather than maintaining a fixed output, yields the most perceptually stable cold sensations, while a reduced actuator configuration closely matches the full hand in spatial coverage and subjective realism. A follow-up study con-firmed that palmar-only actuation can evoke cold sensations on the dorsal side, allowing further hardware reductions. SWIMVR demonstrates that timing and spatial targeting of multimodal cues can produce strong subjective impressions of water interaction in VR, offering a pathway towards scalable, untethered haptic feedback for immersive environments.

title: DreamAnywhere: Object-Centric Panoramic 3D Scene Generation
authors: Edoardo A. Dominici, Jozef Hladky, Floor Verhoeven, Lukas Radl, Thomas Deixelberger, Stefan Ainetter, Philipp Drescher, Stefan Hauswiesner, Arno Coomans, Giacomo Nazzaro, Konstantinos Vardis, Markus Steinberger
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Recent advances in text-to-3D scene generation have demonstrated significant potential to transform content creation across multiple industries. Although the research community has made impressive progress in addressing the challenges of this complex task, existing methods often generate environments that are only front-facing, lack visual fidelity, exhibit limited scene understanding, and are typically finetuned for either indoor or outdoor settings. In this work, we address these issues and propose DreamAnywhere , a modular system for the fast generation and prototyping of 3D scenes. Our system synthesizes a 360° panoramic image from text, decomposes it into background and objects, constructs a complete 3D representation through hybrid inpainting, and lifts object masks to detailed 3D objects that are placed in the virtual environment. DreamAnywhere supports immersive navigation and intuitive object-level editing, making it ideal for scene exploration, visual mock-ups, and rapid prototyping—all with minimal manual modeling. These features make our system particularly suitable for low-budget movie production, enabling quick iteration on scene layout and visual tone without the overhead of traditional 3D workflows. Our modular pipeline is highly customizable as it allows components to be replaced independently. Compared to current state-of-the-art text- and image-based 3D scene generation approaches, DreamAnywhere shows significant improvements in coherence in novel view synthesis and achieves competitive image quality, demonstrating its effectiveness across diverse and challenging scenarios. A comprehensive user study demonstrates a clear preference for our method over existing approaches, validating both its technical robustness and practical usefulness.

title: Hybrid Circuit and Measurement-based Quantum Computing Architecture with Encoded-Fusion-Assisted Teleportation
authors: Harashta Tatimma Larasati, Byung-Soo Choi
abstract: Circuit-based quantum computing (CBQC) has far remained the most intuitive and experimentally mature model of universal quantum computation, serving as the basis for today’s noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices and emerging fault-tolerant quantum computation (FTQC) due to its well-developed control and error-correction techniques. Nevertheless, scalability challenges and suitability to photonic platforms have motivated the exploration of alternative computational models such as measurement-based quantum computing (MBQC), which separates entanglement generation from actual computation and is considered more natural for scalable and distributed architectures. More recently, fusion-based quantum computation (FBQC), and in particular its encoded variant (EFBQC), have been proposed to alleviate many of the practical limitations of traditional MBQC by using constant-size resource states and encoded fusion operations with controllable failure behavior, thus improving practicality, flexibility, and fault-tolerance. Motivated by these potential advantages of measurement-based approaches while recognizing the continued prominence and circuit-level versatility of CBQC, in this work, we propose a hybrid approach that combines CBQC with (E)FBQC to improve long-range connectivity and Clifford-dominated subroutine execution under threshold-compatible operating conditions, enabling higher performance in the context of fault tolerance and architectural scalability. In detail, we propose an encoded-fusion- assisted teleportation (EFAT) mechanism, with two configurations for different scenarios: (1) the Minimal Hybrid, in which (E)FBQC acts as a logical entanglement bus that supplies encoded Bell pairs for teleportation-based connectivity between CBQC modules, thus replacing distance-dependent routing with teleportation and reducing routing overhead when routing dominates the logical schedule, while remaining compatible with the derived surface-code threshold condition; (2) the Extended Hybrid, in which (E)FBQC additionally participates in the computation alongside CBQC nodes, so that encoded cluster fragments can be used to execute Clifford subroutines in constant depth. Our performance analysis shows that the Minimal Hybrid can reduce routing-dominated logical depth while remaining compatible with the derived fault-tolerance condition, whereas the Extended Hybrid can further compress Clifford-dominated subroutines and may improve the temporal scheduling of magic-state supply, at the expense of higher photonic throughput and tighter synchronization requirements. Together, these results indicate a practical route toward scalable, modular, and fault-tolerant quantum computing based on encoded-fusion-assisted hybrid architectures.

title: Learning High-Order Relationships with Hypergraph Attention-based Spatio-Temporal Aggregation for Brain Disease Analysis
authors: Wenqi Hu, Xuerui Su, Guanliang Li, Yidi Pan, Aijing Lin
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Functional connectivity derived from functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) primarily captures pairwise interactions between brain regions, which limits its ability to characterize complex high-order relationships. Hypergraph-based methods provide a natural way to model such interactions, yet most existing approaches rely on predefined hypergraph structures and neglect temporal dynamics, resulting in limited expressiveness and interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework that jointly learns informative and sparse high-order brain structures along with their temporal dynamics. Inspired by the information bottleneck principle, we introduce an objective that maximizes information and minimizes redundancy, aiming to retain disease-relevant high-order features while suppressing irrelevant information. The proposed framework consists of three key components: (1) a multi-hyperedge binary mask module for hypergraph structure learning, (2) a hypergraph self-attention aggregation module that captures spatial features through adaptive attention across nodes and hyperedges, and (3) a spatio-temporal low-dimensional network for extracting discriminative spatio-temporal representations for disease classification. Experiments on benchmark fMRI datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches and effectively captures meaningful high-order brain interactions. These findings provide new insights into brain network modeling, showing potential for analyzing neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: Generative AI for ransomware identification and mitigation: Taxonomy, challenges, and future directions
authors: Erick D. Ramirez-Martinez, Jesús A. Pérez-D-az, Noe M. Yungaicela-Naula
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Ransomware is a relevant threat that is causing losses in millions and affecting several organizations worldwide. Emerging actors leverage strategies such as living-off-the-land, obfuscation, and fileless malware to perform increasingly sophisticated attacks. Generative AI is now being considered as a tool for combating cyberattacks, but its real-world effectiveness, risks, and deployment trade-offs are still unclear in the field of ransomware, pointing to the need for a thorough, rigorous review. This review paper investigates the application of generative AI techniques for the detection and mitigation of ransomware attacks. To this end, we introduce a taxonomy for categorizing the solutions presented in recent studies, thereby offering deeper insights, detecting challenges, and proposing research directions. We also discuss misalignment between academia and industry solutions given the current ransomware landscape. This research presents a first analysis of transformer models and generative adversarial networks leveraged exclusively to detect and mitigate ransomware. Our review reveals that there is a need for dedicated and up-to-date ransomware datasets, new detection strategies, and new data for static and dynamic analysis.

title: Algorithm–Hardware Co-Design of Digital Compute-in-Memory Architecture Supporting Flexible and Temporal N:M Sparsity
authors: Akshat Ramachandran, Souvik Kundu, Arnab Raha, Shamik Kundu, Deepak A. Mathaikutty, Tushar Krishna
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Structured pruning with fixed N:M sparsity ratios in large language models (LLMs) significantly constrains model expressivity, often leading to suboptimal accuracy. While supporting multiple N:M configurations can enhance representational flexibility, such a capability typically introduces substantial hardware complexity and overhead. To overcome these limitations, we first present FLOW, a flexible, layer-wise, outlier-density-aware N:M sparsity selection framework. FLOW adaptively determines the optimal N and M values per layer within a specified range by jointly considering the magnitude and distribution of outliers, thereby improving sparsity allocation and model fidelity. Extending this idea, we introduce FLOW++, which generalizes flexible N:M sparsity to the temporal domain for reasoning LLMs. FLOW++ enables temporal pruning by dynamically adapting sparsity patterns across reasoning thought types. To support efficient deployment of models with such dynamically varying sparsity patterns, we propose FlexCiM, a flexible, low-overhead, digital compute-in-memory (DCiM) architecture. FlexCiM partitions the DCiM macro into smaller submacros, which are adaptively aggregated and disaggregated through distribution and merging mechanisms for different values of N and M. We conduct experiments across different LLM families and state-space models and conclusively demonstrate that the proposed algorithm-hardware co-design framework achieves up to 36% higher accuracy, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.75\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> faster inference, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.5\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> lower energy consumption compared to existing alternatives, establishing an effective balance between flexibility and hardware efficiency in sparse LLM inference. Code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/FLOW-open-project/FLOW</uri>

title: Sensing-Assisted Secure Communication in MA-Aided ISAC: CRB Analysis and Robust Design
authors: Yaxuan Chen, Guangchi Zhang, Miao Cui, Hao Fu, Qingqing Wu, Rui Zhang
abstract: core challenge in physical-layer security is the difficulty of obtaining the channel state information (CSI) of potential eavesdroppers. The inherent sensing functionality of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems offers a promising solution by enabling the estimation of key parameters, such as the eavesdropper’s angles of departure (AoDs). Capitalizing on this capability, we propose a sensing-assisted secure communication scheme for a movable antenna (MA)-aided ISAC system. The scheme comprises two stages: eavesdropper AoD sensing and secure communication. In the first stage, the base station (BS) optimizes the positions of its transmit and receive MAs to enhance sensing accuracy. We derive the closed-form Cramèr-Rao bound (CRB) for the estimated AoDs to fundamentally characterize how MA positions influence the estimation uncertainty. In the second stage, the BS ensures secure communication by designing a robust beamforming vector that accounts for the AoD uncertainty region and by further optimizing the transmit MAs’ positions to maximize the secrecy rate. To manage the end-to-end design, we formulate a joint optimization problem. This intractable non-convex problem is decomposed into two subproblems. For the first subproblem, we develop an alternating optimization (AO) algorithm to solve the CRB minimization problem. For the second subproblem, we solve the worst-case secrecy rate maximization problem using a method based on backward induction, convex hull construction, and AO. Finally, simulation results are provided to demonstrate the significant advantages of the proposed scheme compared to various benchmarks.

title: Orchestrating Space-Air-Ground Integrated Networks for Ubiquitous 6G V2X
authors: Anal Paul, Mayur Katwe, Abhinav Singh Parihar
abstract: As the automotive industry moves toward Level 4/5 autonomy, continuous vehicle-to-everything (V2X) connectivity becomes a safety-critical requirement rather than a convenience. Terrestrial cellular V2X (C-V2X) remains constrained by economic deployment limits, leaving persistent coverage gaps along rural highways and remote corridors. We examine how non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), including low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites and high-altitude platform stations (HAPS), converge with terrestrial infrastructure in a space–air–ground integrated network (SAGIN) for 6G V2X. We trace the 3GPP evolution from Release 17 NR-based NTN with transparent payloads to Release 19 regenerative architectures with on-board processing, and analyze key engineering challenges such as Doppler dynamics under double mobility, large round-trip times, and link-budget constraints with vehicle-class antennas. A system-level simulation indicates that SAGIN-assisted V2X can reduce handover failure rates by more than an order of magnitude at highway speeds and substantially tighten the long latency tail of fragmented terrestrial deployments, and we outline how AI/ML-driven orchestration across space, air, and ground can enable a practical NTN-V2X roadmap for 6G.

title: A Self-Consistent Energy Transfer Matrix Method for Stratified Anisotropic Media
authors: Jiuyang Fan, Yuxian Zhang, Liangshuai Guo, Hongxia Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: To address the computational challenges associated with determining the vertical polarization propagation coefficients in the traditional transfer matrix method (T-TMM) and the resulting energy non-conservation issue, a self-consistent energy TMM (SCE-TMM) is proposed in this paper. Based on the vector wave equation of anisotropies, this research derives its corresponding eigenvalues through an analytical method. A transfer matrix model is then applied to describe plane wave propagation in stratified media, yielding reflection and transmission coefficients for transverse electric (TE) and transverse magnetic (TM) modes along the horizontal direction with appropriate phase correction. The vertical propagation coefficients are obtained through inverse derivation from the horizontal propagation coefficients combined with the wave equation. The validation of the model using lossless configurations, specifically an optical-frequency single-layer β-BaB₂O₄ (BBO) crystal and a microwave-frequency multilayer anisotropic structure, confirms its strict compliance with the principle of energy conservation. Comparative studies with the finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method and COMSOL Multiphysics simulations demonstrate the significant computational efficiency advantages of the proposed SCE-TMM. Finally, we design a far-infrared absorber based on anisotropic black phosphorus (BP), illustrating the potential of SCE-TMM for innovative applications in optical device design.

title: A Low-Communication FDFD Solver for Distributed Parallel Electromagnetic Simulation
authors: Zihao Ma, Bin Zou, Eng Leong Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a low-communication finite-difference frequency-domain (LC-FDFD) solver for distributed parallel simulation of large-scale three-dimensional scattering problems. We propose a customized matrix reordering scheme that preserves spatial locality of field components and transforms the FDFD system into a compact banded structure. Based on the reordering scheme, a hybrid iterative-direct solver based on the SPIKE algorithm is proposed to partition the global system into independent subproblems solvable in parallel across multiple GPUs in a distributed cluster. When solving reordered FDFD systems with wide bandwidth and in-band sparsity, the proposed method avoids explicit spike matrix construction by using a matrix-free technique, thereby significantly reducing both computational cost and memory requirements. Several numerical experiments are presented, whose results show excellent efficiency and scalability for large-scale electromagnetic simulations.

title: ICoECIT 2026 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)

title: Conference Organization: SECE 2025
authors: N/A

title: UAV-Aided NOMA Network with Artificial Noise Against Internal and External Eavesdropping
authors: Peishun Yan, Zhanghua Cao, Bin Li, Yulong Zou, Chunguo Li, Miaowen Wen, Arumugam Nallanathan
abstract: This paper studies the physical-layer security (PLS) for an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-aided non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) network, where transmissions from a base station (BS) to a trusted near user (TNU) and an untrusted far user (UFU) are assisted by an UAV relay in the face of multiple non-colluding external eavesdroppers (Es). The traditional strategies such as artificial noise-aided friendly jammer (TAN-FJ), artificial noise with non-friendly jammer (TAN-NFJ), and reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-aided schemes fail to simultaneously guarantee the secrecy for both TNU and UFU, thus how to guarantee the PLS for NOMA users becomes an urgent issue to be solved. In this context, we propose an artificial noise (PAN) scheme aided with digital network coding (DNC). Specifically, the artificial noise signals generated by UAV are resorted to encrypt confidential signals with the assistance of DNC, which realizes one-time pad, thus the PLS of TNU and UFU can be ensured. We resort security-reliability tradeoff (SRT) as a metric to evaluate the PLS for our proposed PAN scheme. Hence, the exact and asymptotic expressions of outage probability (OP) and intercept probability (IP) are derived to quantify the reliability and security. Moreover, numerical simulations validate the correctness of our theoretical results and reveal that: 1) the PAN scheme enhances the SRT performance of near user compared to the TAN-FJ, TAN-NFJ, and RIS-aided schemes, while far user by the PAN scheme achieves almost the same SRT performance as the TAN-FJ scheme and significantly better performance than the TAN-NFJ and RIS-aided schemes, which indicates that the PAN scheme realizes the fairness of secrecy transmissions for NOMA users; 2) the SRT performance of TNU benefits from multiple antennas at BS and UAV, which has ignorable effect on the SRT performance of UFU; 3) the antenna number at UAV has more obvious impact on the SRT of TUN than the antenna number at BS.

title: 2025 2nd International Conference on Emerging Trends in Electronic Devices and Computational Techniques (EDCT)
authors: N/A

title: T2LF: LLM-Guided Multimodal Diffusion for Text-to-Light Field Synthesis
authors: Soyoung Yoon, Namhyuk Ahn, In Kyu Park
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: We present a novel text-driven approach for light field (LF) synthesis. Existing methods typically generate LFs from given images, requiring users to find reference images, which makes it difficult to construct the desired scene directly and limits scene diversity. Moreover, existing methods are mainly designed for limited baselines from training datasets, making it difficult to implement various viewpoint changes and consequently limiting the flexibility of motion. In contrast, our method directly synthesizes LFs from user-provided text descriptions by leveraging the scene understanding capabilities of a multi-modal large language model (LLM) and the generative power of a diffusion model. Given a text prompt describing the desired LF, the multimodal LLM extracts relevant information for LF synthesis, which then guides a diffusion model to produce diverse scenes and motions. This approach enables LF synthesis even with a pre-trained model not initially designed for this purpose, requiring only minimal fine-tuning. The proposed framework enables visually diverse LF synthesis with only text input. Experimental results demonstrate that the synthesized LFs exhibit geometric consistency and achieve advanced synthesis quality compared to existing methods.

title: A hybrid machine learning and gamified framework for effective student engagement in a metaverse learning environment
authors: S.Adinarayana, Daruru Sarika, BVN Prasad Paruchuri, K Yogeswara Rao, U. Sesadri, S.Hrushikesava Raju
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: The shortcomings of traditional approaches in metaverse learning suffers in interactive learning, a lack of motivation, follow a one-size-fits-all style, poor long-term retention, and a static classroom. Moreover, these are difficult to scale to remote learners, etc. To overcome these challenges, metaverse learning is adopted, with immersive opportunities created, addressing technological and ethical concerns by embedding elements such as quests, rewards, real-time feedback, and virtual collaboration by integrating with gamification. To ensure improved education outcomes, the cognitive, emotional, and behavioral engagement features are focused among learners, resulting in. The gamification in metaverse learning, along with machine learning approaches such as Random Forest, LSTM, and Gradient Boosting, provides a transformative approach to modern education, aiming to enhance student engagement, motivation, and knowledge retention. This study motivates that gamified mechanisms within the Metaverse can influence students’ cognitive, emotional, and behavioral engagement, whereas ML approaches enhance prediction analytics on engagement levels, and improve based on timely feedback. This study motivates developers and educators to use the metaverse to create dynamic, inclusive, and student-centered learning ecosystems. The domain metaverse, in which gamification would reshape the education system, with active learning promotion, and expand the use of the digital era and its interconnected means.

title: Improvise, Adapt, Overcome — Telescopic Adapters for Efficient Fine-tuning of Vision Language Models in Medical Imaging
authors: Ujjwal Mishra, Vinita Shukla, Praful Hambarde, Amit Shukla
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Adapting Vision Language Segmentation Models (VLSMs) to medical imaging domains requires significant computational overhead when using conventional fine-tuning approaches. Existing Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods apply uniform adapter dimensions across all transformer layers, leading to suboptimal parameter allocation and reduced adaptation efficiency. We introduce Telescopic Adapters, a novel PEFT framework that employs depth-aware scaling to progressively increase adapter capacity from shallow to deep transformer layers. Our method integrates lightweight bottleneck modules within CLIPSeg’s vision and text encoders, with adapter dimensions dynamically scaled based on layer depth and semantic relevance. Using only 613k trainable parameters—244× fewer than end-to-end fine-tuning, Telescopic Adapters achieve superior performance across five diverse medical datasets spanning polyp segmentation, skin lesion detection, and breast ultrasound imaging. Comprehensive ablation studies demonstrate that deeper layers require substantially more adaptation capacity than shallow layers, validating our telescopic scaling hypothesis. Our approach establishes a new paradigm for efficient medical VLSM fine-tuning, enabling deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments while maintaining competitive segmentation accuracy.

title: Causality-Driven Audits of Model Robustness
authors: Nathan Drenkow, William Paul, Chris Ribaudo, Mathias Unberath
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Robustness audits of deep neural networks (DNN) provide a means to uncover model sensitivities to the challenging real-world imaging conditions that significantly degrade DNN performance in-the-wild. Such conditions are often the result of multiple interacting factors inherent to the environment, sensor, or processing pipeline and may lead to complex image distortions that are not easily categorized. When robustness audits are limited to a set of isolated imaging effects or distortions, the results cannot be (easily) transferred to real-world conditions where image corruptions may be more complex or nuanced. To address this challenge, we present a new alternative robustness auditing method that uses causal inference to measure DNN sensitivities to the factors of the imaging process that cause complex distortions. Our approach uses causal models to explicitly encode assumptions about the domain-relevant factors and their interactions. Then, through extensive experiments on natural and rendered images across multiple vision tasks, we show that our approach reliably estimates causal effects of each factor on DNN performance using only observational domain data. These causal effects directly tie DNN sensitivities to observable properties of the imaging pipeline in the domain of interest towards reducing the risk of unexpected DNN failures when deployed in that domain.

title: Evaluation of Rendering Strategies for Supporting Recognition of Raised Pin Shape Geometries
authors: MacKenzie Harnett, Kaitlyn Hawkins, Rebecca F. Friesen
abstract: Refreshable pin displays are promising tools for communicating haptic shapes without the high storage and manufacturing time requirements of analog materials, such as braille embossers or 3-D printing. Even as this technology develops and comes down in price, however, there is a dearth of studies on the design of the tactile graphics rendered on these displays, and necessary integrations (such as multi-height functionalities) for effective graphic rendering are underreviewed. This paper explores methods of improving tactile shape comprehension on a pin array, and presents the results of a user study where participants were asked to identify a set of six different shape primitives using six different rendering strategies. These strategies included: raised outlines of shapes, raised filled shapes, recessed outlines of shapes, recessed filled shapes, raised outlines with accentuated corners, and raised outlines adhered with a rubber cover acting as a 'low pass filter'. The results of this study indicated no clear advantage of one rendering strategy over the others in terms of identification rates; however, we observed that exploration strategies tended to shift from shape visualization to counting, depending on the rendering strategy implemented, as indicated by the type of identification errors participants made.

title: Multi Temporal Change Detection for Flood Impact Assessment Using VISH Model and Remote Sensing Technique
authors: Pragynaparamita Nayak, Chinmayee Pati
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Floods are one of the most destructive natural disasters, especially in mountainous regions like Sikkim, where intense rainfall and sudden glacial lake outburst events can trigger rapid flooding. In October 2023, Sikkim experienced a severe flood that caused widespread damage to lives, agriculture, and infrastructure. In this work, we focuses on multi temporal change detection and impact assessment of this flood using satellite remote sensing and GIS data. Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 optical images from pre flood and post flood periods were analyzed to identify changes in surface water and map inundated areas. To improve the reliability of flood mapping, a VISH (Variogram Integrated Satellite Hydrodynamic) model was applied, which integrates geostatistical variogram analysis, multi source satellite data, terrain information from DEM, and hydrodynamic flow behaviour. This approach helps in capturing the spatial patterns and dynamics of flooding. The derived flood extent maps were further integrated with land use and land cover and population datasets in a GIS environment to assess impacts on agricultural land, settlements, and vulnerable districts. An accuracy assessment using high resolution reference data was also conducted to validate the results. The study demonstrates that combining multi temporal satellite data, GIS, and the VISH model provides an effective framework for flood change analysis and impact assessment, supporting disaster management and planning in fragile Himalayan regions.

title: Medical Image Segmentation Using Enhanced U-Net Architecture for the Chaos CT Dataset
authors: Wuit Hmone Oo, Phyu Myo Thwe
abstract: Accurate segmentation of abdominal organs is critical in medical diagnosis, surgical planning, and disease monitoring. Conventional manual segmentation methods are time-consuming and prone to human error, motivating the adoption of deep learning-based automated approaches. In this study, an Enhanced U-Net architecture is developed to improve upon the standard U-Net architecture by incorporating a self-attention mechanism at the bottleneck layer to enhance spatial feature representation and capture long-range contextual dependencies between pixels. The segmentation task uses the CHAOS-CT dataset which includes annotated CT images of abdominal organs such as the liver, kidneys, and spleen, was used to train and evaluate the model. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model architecture achieves superior segmentation accuracy compared to the baseline model, with improvements observed in Dice Similarity Coefficient, Intersection over Union, and precision metrices. The findings indicate that the integration of self-attention mechanisms significantly enhances the model ability to delineate organ boundaries and improves overall segmentation performance for abdominal CT images.

title: Emotimate - AI Companion Agent for Mental Health Using Multimodal Generative AI
authors: Ayush Kadam, Devansh Patel, Dhrish Shah, Ami Munshi, Sapna Shah
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Emotimate is a multimodal AI emotional care bot to address the demand for an emotionally intelligent, situationally aware, and psychologically safe AI emotional care companion. Mental health chatbots prioritize clinical safety over emotional continuity. Emotimate solves this by using the best Gemini 1.5 Pro LLM, structured SQL mood detection, and weights for multimodal fusion logic. The framework also supports weight calibration and timestamps for DB and realtime queries. Improved dialog management with new Gemini 1.5 Pro prompt templates for contextual empathy and latency management.Instantaneous mood recognition and autonomous dialogue flow are provided. Empathetic responses are generated by the Dialogue Engine while Safety Core, through the fine-tuning of BERT-Sentiment and DistilRoBERTa, identifies emotional distress and triggers escalation. A speech-to-text system that identifies seven vocal expressions expands the scope of multimodal emotion detection and enhances escalation prediction. Mood trend analysis is supported by a PostgreSQL relational database (Users, Conversations, Mood Log), user sessions are protected with SHA-256 hashed credentials and subsequent linked with qualitative feedback gathered via user satisfaction surveys on core quantifiable aspects (safety, empathy tagging, escalation). The findings suggest that LLM-based multimodal systems, like Emotimate, can augment emotional support safely, with privacy, ethicality, and sustained trust from users. Incorporation of dynamic weight editing and server-side timestamp normalization usability enhancement for administrators and boosting realtime emotional analytics accuracy.

title: Machine Learning in Wireless Sensor Networks: Techniques and Performance Trade-Offs
authors: Chandra Naik, Apoorva Nayak, Anushree Dhanashetti, Anujna N, Bhagyashri Math
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) form an essential component of modern intelligent infrastructures, supporting domains such as healthcare, environmental monitoring, industrial automation, and smart urban environments. However, challenges such as limited battery power, processing capacity, latency, and security vulnerabilities hinder their performance. Machine Learning (ML) techniques have proven to be efficient tools for addressing these constraints.ML models improve routing efficiency, detect anomalies, and predict node failures. Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning methods enhance adaptability and energy management in dynamic environments. Evaluations of these models are typically conducted using simulators such as NS-2, NS-3, OMNeT++, and MATLAB, focusing on parameters such as energy efficiency, latency, and network lifetime.This paper highlights current advancements, identifies challenges such as real-time learning, scalability, and secure data sharing, and explores future directions including TinyML, federated learning, and edge intelligence for building efficient and sustainable WSNs.

title: Zero-Lag Invisibility: A Deep Learning Framework for Instantaneous Human Removal in Live Video
authors: Tanisha Bagchi, Apurba Nandi, Sangita Dutta, Avik Kumar Das, Uddipan Ghosh, Arijeet Ghosh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: In this work, we present an invisibility framework, a real-time system designed to remove a human subject from live video streams while maintaining perfect temporal synchronization. Unlike traditional chroma keying or background subtraction techniques that rely on static settings, the proposed method adapts dynamically to environmental changes. It integrates deep semantic segmentation with an adaptive background buffer and edge-aware mask refinement to ensure precise and seamless invisibility. A scene-change detection module enables automatic background adjustment, preventing ghosting and lag. The zero-buffer, GPU-accelerated design delivers smooth performance at 60 fps with minimal latency, producing visually coherent results in diverse conditions. This approach demonstrates a robust balance between accuracy, adaptability, and computational efficiency, establishing a strong foundation for real-time applications in virtual communication, augmented reality, and interactive media.

title: Comparative Analysis of Deep Learning Approaches for SDN Intrusion Detection System
authors: Shubham Yadav, Pushpendra Kumar Chandra, Satish Kumar Negi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: The rapid expansion of cloud computing, Internet of Things, and next generation mobile communication technologies has significantly increased the complexity and scale of modern communication networks. Software Defined Networking has emerged as a promising paradigm to address these challenges by enabling centralized control, programmability, and flexible network management. However, the centralized nature of the SDN controller also introduces critical security vulnerabilities, making SDN infrastructures highly susceptible to attacks such as Distributed Denial of Service and spoofing. To address these concerns, this paper presents a comparative evaluation of machine learning and deep learning based Intrusion Detection Systems for SDN environments using the InSDN dataset, which reflects realistic traffic behavior and attack scenarios. The study focuses on analyzing detection performance, class imbalance handling, and computational efficiency across multiple models. In addition, a Convolutional Neural Network based intrusion detection framework is implemented and evaluated to assess its suitability for deployment in dynamic SDN based networks. The experimental results highlight the trade-off between detection accuracy and computational overhead, providing insights for selecting efficient intrusion detection models for practical SDN deployments.

title: Quantum-Classical Integration for Predicting User Engagement in Social Network Advertisements
authors: Amrita Kundu, Samya Muhuri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) is a rapidly emerging paradigm that combines the computational power of quantum systems with the adaptability of machine learning models. In this paper, the practicality of a Variational Quantum Classifier (VQC) for a real-world table dataset, namely the Social Network Ads dataset, is explored. The dataset targets the classification of user purchase intentions based on their demographic and salary profiles. The proposed approach employs a parameterised quantum circuit with data encoded via rotation gates and layer-wise entanglement via a ring connectivity layout. The model is trained in a quantum-classical loop using the PennyLane package on a noiseless simulator, with the binary cross-entropy loss function and the Adam optimiser for parameter updates. Experimental results show that the VQC achieves competitively high classification accuracy and demonstrate the potential of hybrid quantum classifiers for social and commercial analytics applications. Further benchmarking of the proposed results against classical baselines and interpretation of limitations related to scalability and quantum noise have been conducted. This work contributes to the growing body of evidence that near-term quantum models can move beyond artificial benchmarks and deliver insightful results for practical machine learning applications.

title: Mathematical Foundations of Adaptive Fractional Differencing for Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Sarit Maitra, Vivek Mishra, Kapil Arora
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Financial time-series forecasting is a complex, nonstationary and long-range dependent process that challenges the assumptions of existing short-memory models. While fractional difference (FD) offers a satisfactory approach, its practical implementation remains limited due to parameter estimation challenges, computational efficiency, and a lack of integration with ML workflows. This work proposed a mathematically grounded approach consisting of an Adaptive Fractional Differencing framework that contributes three different key innovations. First, a robust estimation procedure, combines wavelet-based Hurst Exponent analysis with regularized regression to reduce smallsample bias. Secondly, a data-adaptive truncation mechanism for operationalizing the infinite FD series via cross-validation and the third approach works on a theoretical pathway for embedding FDtransformed features into modern ML pipelines. This work bridges the gap between classical time-series and modern datadriven methods, offering a scalable and rigorous preprocessing foundation for future empirical research.

title: Highly Sensitive Tunnelling Magnetoresistance-Based Positioning Systems
authors: Mohamad Idris Wani, Brij Kishor Katare, Meraj Ahmad, Huxi Wang, Hadi Heidari, Benish Jan, Shahid Malik
abstract: Magnetoresistance (MR) sensors are employed in highly sensitive and precise position applications due to their high sensitivity. AMR and GMR sensors show great promise in this field; however, TMR is rapidly replacing other MR sensors owing to significant improvements in sensitivity and range, while maintaining a large bandwidth. However, TMR sensors, like other MR sensors, have a very high flicker noise content, which hampers their performance. In this article, a highly sensitive TMR sensor, TMR9082, is examined, and its suitability for nanometer and sub-nanometer positioning systems is explored. A complete process flow for selecting a low-dropout regulator (LDO) and an INA to minimise noise contribution and maximise resolution in a positioning system is shown. The total noise at the output of the INA with a gain of 10 is calculated to be 166.34 µV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf> for a bandwidth of 100 kHz. This calculation is verified using a custom-made TMR9082 sensor model and simulated in LT Spice. Additionally, using the referred-to input noise, the minimum detectable magnetic field is calculated to be 5.44 nT. Based on the total noise, an ADC selection section is also provided.

title: Privacy-Preserving Strategies in AI-Based Applications
authors: Pramila P. Shinde, Vidyullata V. Devmane
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: The fast integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into all applications has significantly enhanced the personalization and quality of human life. It came with the need to collect and process large volumes of data. The majority of the data collected are considered personal and sensitive. One needs to understand that various applications cannot ensure a high-quality of data protection. Although general regulations need to be adhered to for developing applications so that it protects personal data and consequently achieves general trustworthiness among users on data protection for AI-based applications. In the recent past, there have been numerous research papers which have pointed out AI-based applications vulnerability to data privacy breaches and Byzantine attacks. But it still remains a challenging task to address such threats, as previous defense mechanisms are inadequate to get Byzantine resilience, data privacy, and better AI model training accuracy.The research papers have contributed to several privacy preserving strategies such as homomorphic encryption, differential privacy, and secure multi-party computation. The previous study also outlines commonly used datasets, evaluation metrics. Hence, it is evident that robust privacy preserving techniques, and advanced AI models are the necessity of developing secure, trustworthy AI based applications. These findings further serve as an aid for future innovations aimed at responsible and user-centric AI based application development. This paper systematically investigates various AI based applications with the primary objective of analyzing attacks that breaches data privacy and privacy preserving strategies used in consideration with datasets and evaluation metrics employed. It also highlights implementation of RSA algorithm on image files.

title: Leveraging Deep Neural Networks for Enhanced Otitis Media Diagnosis
authors: Navyansh Kothari, Pushpansh Mishra, Nimisha Malekar, Anushka Srivastava, Shruti Mishra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Otitis media (OM) is a prevalent spectrum of middle ear inflammatory conditions and one of the most prevalent medical conditions, particularly in children. The majority of it is diagnosed through otoscopic images—a subjective and error-inclined process. Specialized diagnostic apparatuses are costly, with limited availability and reproducibility. The paper proposes a novel approach to eardrum infection diagnosis through deep learning and multimodal data analysis. It employs a scalable ensemble of four state-of-the-art models: RegNet-X 16GF and 3.2GF for highresolution feature extraction, MobileNetV2 for light-weight real-time processing, and ResNeXt50 32×4d for advanced pattern recognition. It was trained on heterogeneous otoscopic images with demographics, symptoms, and medical histories added and achieved a 92.56% accuracy—higher than single models (MobileNetV2: 89.2%, ResNeXt50: 91.5%, RegNet-X 16GF: 90.8%, RegNet-X 3.2GF: 90.1%) and benchmarks like VGG16 and DenseNet121. It demonstrated 96.12% sensitivity, 87.78% specificity, and an F1 score of 87.94%. It was able to distinguish between conditions with overlapping visual features and decreased false positives, especially in chronic OM and earwax blockage. With computational efficiency, scalability, and clinical practicability, the model presents a trustworthy diagnostic tool for early treatment and improved outcomes, especially in resource-scarce environments.

title: Towards Photorealistic Style Transfer with Multimodal Guidance and Robustness to Content Images in Arbitrary Styles
authors: Ruikai Zhou, Yating Liu, Yi Xu
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Existing photorealistic style transfer methods are broadly categorized into two groups: image-guided and text-guided approaches. The image-guided paradigm requires a reference style image, which is superior when the target style is difficult to define precisely with text. Unfortunately, such references are not always available in practical scenarios. In contrast, the text-guided paradigm offers greater flexibility. However, existing text-guided methods often fail to preserve the details of content images or perform poorly when content images deviate from normal style. In this paper, we present a novel multimodal-guided photorealistic style transfer framework, supporting flexible switching and fusion of both modalities while ensuring robust performance across content images in arbitrary styles. Specifically, we adopt a two-stage pipeline. First, the Style Removal Module removes the original style from the content image. Then, the Style Injection Module applies stylization based on the style guidance (image, text, or their fusion). To make the text-guided branch compatible with this pipeline, we propose the Image-Assisted Textual Style Injection (IATSI) strategy. Additionally, we design a Dual-Residual Adaptive MLP (DRA-MLP), which exhibits strong color mapping capability and avoids spatial distortions. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in both image-guided and text-guided settings. Moreover, we innovatively implement multimodal fusion-guided photorealistic style transfer, achieving promising results.

title: Attention-Driven BiLSTM with Deeplift Explanations for Robust Cloud-Based DDoS Detection
authors: Geetha K, Belide Nikhil Chandan, Sri Surya Shobith Kamisetty, A Sohan Avaneesh Guptha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: DDoS attacks are considered to be a significant risk to cloud infrastructure since they have the ability to slow down services through saturation of the network resources. Real-time detection of such attacks is a serious issue due to high dimensionality and dynamism of modern network traffic. In this paper, a hybrid Attention-based Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Attention-BiLSTM) model with a DeepLift enhancement to explainable DDoS detection has been proposed. The BiLSTM part is used to detect intricate order patterns, and the attention mechanism identifies important features that lead to classification. DeepLift also extends the architecture, offering the feature attribution that is transparent and interpretable, allowing one to learn about the decision-making process of the model. Experimental tests using benchmark data of DDoS show that the model performs better with an accuracy of 99.91 and an almost perfect precision, recall, and F1-score. The effectiveness of the new method is described relative to the benchmark models like CNN, RNN, SVM and Logistic Regression in identifying the accuracy and interpretability. This paper highlights the possibility of combining deep learning and explainable artificial intelligence to develop resilient, straightforward and extensible intrusion detection frameworks in dynamically arranged cloud networks.

title: NSST-Based Multi-Modal Medical Image Fusion With LiqDRY-Net Architecture
authors: Fahimuddin Shaik, Peddapullaiahgari Hariobulesu, C.H. NagaRaju, Charitha Kummetha, Gopanapalli Chidvilas, Kovuru Aswini, Ummadi Gowtham Reddy
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: A Liquid Rybak Neural Network LiqDRYNN or dual channel multimodal image fusion is used in this study as LiqDRY-Net nonsubsampled shearlet transform (NSST) space, is suggested to be used to do Liquid Fusion. The framework takes benefit of the generality of the liquid neural dynamics based on a biologically analogous Rybak Neural Network to combine complementary data between MRI, PET and SPECT data.
